source.subject: Quick restart — two fast ways to reconnect
messages:
From: Alex Martinez <alex.martinez@redwood.com>
To: Jordan Liu <jordan.liu@scaleapp.io>
Cc: Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwood.com>
Date: Tue, 28 May 2029 16:02:00 -0700
Subject: Quick restart — two fast ways to reconnect

Hey Jordan,

Sorry we missed you on the discovery call just now — totally understand that things pop. Two quick ways we can get you back up to speed so you can evaluate without a big time commitment: 

1) Book a focused 15-minute refresh with me (live walkthrough + quick Q&A): https://calendly.com/redwood/alex-15min

2) Dive into our sandbox (no install) and a 2-minute video tour I can send over — try a few sample prompts with your data profile: https://sandbox.redwood.ai/scaleapp-tryout

If helpful, I attached a one-page discovery agenda so you can pick what you want to cover. If you prefer, I can loop in Ben (AE) for a short 20–30 minute session to discuss pricing and SLAs.

Two quick asks so I route you correctly: which path do you prefer (1 or 2)? If 1, any preferred windows tomorrow or Thursday morning PT and I’ll send an invite.

Thanks — Alex

--
Alex Martinez
Sales Development Rep, Redwood Inference
alex.martinez@redwood.com | +1 (415) 555-0192
Redwood Inference
Attachment: discovery-agenda.pdf (1 page)
From: Jordan Liu <jordan.liu@scaleapp.io>
To: Alex Martinez <alex.martinez@redwood.com>
Date: Tue, 28 May 2029 16:35:12 -0700
Subject: Re: Quick restart — two fast ways to reconnect

Hey Alex — apologies, an internal outage pulled me into an emergency and I had to step out. Thank you for the follow-up.

I’m actually leaning toward path 2 first (sandbox) so I can poke around before we commit time. If the sandbox looks good I can do a 15-minute call tomorrow at 10:00 AM PT.

Can you send the 2-minute tour video and whatever quickstart notes you have? Also, I’m happy to book the 10:00 slot via your Calendly if it’s still available.

— Jordan
Jordan Liu
Head of Product, ScaleApp
jordan.liu@scaleapp.io | +1 (650) 555-2211
From: Alex Martinez <alex.martinez@redwood.com>
To: Jordan Liu <jordan.liu@scaleapp.io>
Cc: Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwood.com>
Date: Wed, 29 May 2029 09:07:45 -0700
Subject: Re: Quick restart — two fast ways to reconnect

Thanks Jordan — totally makes sense. I’ve attached a 2-minute tour and a tiny quickstart (sandbox-quickstart.txt) to get you going; no account needed for the trial link. If you want to skip straight to a short live session after trying it, 10:00 AM PT tomorrow works on my calendar.

If you’d like Ben on the call to cover pricing/seat counts, he’s cc’d and can join — otherwise I’ll run the 15-minute walkthrough and handle tech fit.

Quick tour: https://youtu.be/short-demo-link (2:05)
Sandbox: https://sandbox.redwood.ai/scaleapp-tryout
Book: https://calendly.com/redwood/alex-15min

Note: I also added a one-page agenda (same discovery-agenda.pdf) — nothing binding, just to help prioritize the 15 minutes.

Alex

On Tue, May 28, 2029 at 4:35 PM Jordan Liu <jordan.liu@scaleapp.io> wrote:
> Hey Alex — apologies, an internal outage pulled me into an emergency and I had to step out. Thank you for the follow-up.
> 
> I’m actually leaning toward path 2 first (sandbox) so I can poke around before we commit time. If the sandbox looks good I can do a 15-minute call tomorrow at 10:00 AM PT.
> 
> Can you send the 2-minute tour video and whatever quickstart notes you have? Also, I’m happy to book the 10:00 slot via your Calendly if it’s still available.
> 
> — Jordan
From: Jordan Liu <jordan.liu@scaleapp.io>
To: Alex Martinez <alex.martinez@redwood.com>
Date: Thu, 30 May 2029 09:10:02 -07:00
Subject: Re: Quick restart — two fast ways to reconnect

Perfect — I tried the sandbox for ~10 minutes and it looks promising. I’ve booked 15 minutes at 10:00 AM PT tomorrow via your Calendly. Invite shows Alex Martinez — see you then.

One small ask for tomorrow: I’ll have my engineer on mute for the first 10 minutes; we may flip to screenshare for a single prompt latency demo if time allows.

Thanks again — Jordan

> On Wed, May 29, 2029 at 9:07 AM Alex Martinez <alex.martinez@redwood.com> wrote:
> > Thanks Jordan — totally makes sense. I’ve attached a 2-minute tour and a tiny quickstart...
> > 
> > Alex
>
Attachment: booking.ics (calendar invite stub)

source.subject: Request: standard token economics & eligible model list
messages:
From: Evan Strauss <evan@pebblehealth.ai>
To: Aisha Rahman <aisha_rahman@redwood.ai>
Cc: Priya Malhotra <priya.malhotra@pebblehealth.ai>
Date: Wed, 8 May 2029 09:12:00 -0700
Subject: Quick ask: hosted token economics + supported models

Hi Aisha,

We're evaluating hosted inference providers for a customer-facing summarization feature. Can you share your standard per-1M token economics (separate input vs output rates) and a short list of the models you support on the hosted API with their recommended use cases (summarization, chat, embeddings)?

Couple of notes on our side:
- Expected starting volume ~15M tokens/month, potentially ramping to ~120M over 6 months.
- Summaries expand content; outputs could be 2-3x inputs on average.
- We care about predictable unit costs and whether you have a recommended model for low-latency summarization at reasonable cost.

I've attached a short doc with a sample traffic breakdown. If you have a short rate sheet or baseline we can use for early budgetary work that'd be great.

Thanks,
Evan
Evan Strauss
Head of Product
PebbleHealth
attachments: pricing_questions.pdf (doc)
From: Aisha Rahman <aisha_rahman@redwood.ai>
To: Evan Strauss <evan@pebblehealth.ai>
Cc: Priya Malhotra <priya.malhotra@pebblehealth.ai>
Date: Wed, 8 May 2029 11:04:00 -0700
Subject: Re: Quick ask: hosted token economics + supported models

Hi Evan — thanks for the context and the traffic sample. Attaching our baseline PDF with standard IO splits and a short model catalog for hosted. High-level answers below to help with budgeting:

Baseline hosted rates (public list / starter tier, no commit):
- Input tokens: $0.45 per 1M tokens
- Output tokens: $0.90 per 1M tokens

Notes:
- We bill input and output separately. Because you expect output expansion (2-3x), modeled cost should assume the output column.
- Small/fast models (use-case: short summaries, high QPS) are cheaper per token but may produce slightly shorter answers; larger models give higher-quality summaries for complex documents.

Short hosted model roster (recommended use cases):
- redwood-compact-1: low-latency summarization, low cost (good for short clinical notes)
- redwood-ada-2: embeddings & light NLU
- redwood-standard-3: balanced cost/quality for summarization and chat
- redwood-xlarge-1: highest quality for long-form summarization and extraction

We've included rough latency / cost guidance per model in the attached PDF. For predictable economics at scale we recommend a committed monthly token tier (see Ben's email below for discounts).

If you can confirm a 6- or 12-month commit range (or a target monthly token band), I can share a proposal with concrete discounted rates and the specific SLA for Dedicated capacity if you need it.

Best,
Aisha Rahman
Account Executive, Redwood Inference
aisha_rahman@redwood.ai
+1 (415) 555-0124
attachments: redwood_baseline_rates_v1.pdf

On Wed, May 8, 2029 at 09:12 AM Evan Strauss <evan@pebblehealth.ai> wrote:
> Hi Aisha,
> 
> We're evaluating hosted inference providers for a customer-facing summarization feature.... (see attached)
From: Ben Carter <ben_carter@redwood.ai>
To: Evan Strauss <evan@pebblehealth.ai>, Priya Malhotra <priya.malhotra@pebblehealth.ai>
Cc: Aisha Rahman <aisha_rahman@redwood.ai>
Date: Thu, 9 May 2029 10:22:00 -0700
Subject: RW proposal: baseline + commit discounts for PebbleHealth (RW-20290508-001)

Hi Evan / Priya — looping in to put some numbers on the table. Thanks Aisha for the intro and baseline.

We can offer the following committed monthly tiers (hosted API, per-1M token rates shown):
- No commit (public): input $0.45 / 1M, output $0.90 / 1M
- 6-month commit (>= 50M tokens/month): input $0.36 / 1M (20% off), output $0.72 / 1M (20% off)
- 12-month commit (>= 100M tokens/month): input $0.30 / 1M (33% off), output $0.60 / 1M (33% off)

Model availability on hosted remains the same across tiers; Dedicated capacity is an option if you need an SLA-backed latency target. We've attached a proposed commit-tier workbook that lays out example monthly invoices at your banded volumes.

Other commercial notes:
- We can include a one-time onboarding credit for pilots (ask Aisha for specifics).
- Standard terms are Redwood's MSP; we can accommodate customer PO and 30/45 day net terms for established customers.

Next steps: if you confirm a target starting monthly band (15M or 50M), I'll produce a short commercial summary and term sheet you can take to procurement.

Ben Carter
Commercial Lead, Redwood Inference
ben_carter@redwood.ai
attachments: RW-proposed-commit-tiers-2029-05-08.xlsx

On Wed, May 8, 2029 at 11:04 AM Aisha Rahman <aisha_rahman@redwood.ai> wrote:
> Hi Evan — thanks for the context and the traffic sample. Attaching our baseline PDF with standard IO splits and a short model catalog for hosted...
From: Evan Strauss <evan@pebblehealth.ai>
To: Ben Carter <ben_carter@redwood.ai>, Aisha Rahman <aisha_rahman@redwood.ai>
Cc: Priya Malhotra <priya.malhotra@pebblehealth.ai>
Date: Thu, 9 May 2029 11:03:00 -0700
Subject: Re: RW proposal: baseline + commit discounts for PebbleHealth (RW-20290508-001)

Ben / Aisha — thanks for the quick turnaround and the workbook. A couple quick items:

- The 6-month commit band looks closest to where we may land after pilot. Can you confirm whether the 6-month discount applies retroactively to pilot usage if we convert within 30 days?
- Procurement will want a short summary of invoicing cadence and whether you accept a single PO that covers the first quarter.
- We also have a data-security questionnaire (SOC 2 + logs). Can you point me to the relevant security pack or contact to start that thread?

Assuming we can get a short procurement-ready summary, we'll route this internally and aim to decide on a pilot in the next 7–10 business days.

Appreciate the clarity — Evan

On Thu, May 9, 2029 at 10:22 AM Ben Carter <ben_carter@redwood.ai> wrote:
> Hi Evan / Priya — looping in to put some numbers on the table. Thanks Aisha for the intro and baseline...

- redwood-ada-2: embeddings & light NLU
- redwood-standard-3: balanced cost/quality for summarization and chat
- redwood-xlarge-1: highest quality for long-form summarization and extraction

We've included rough latency / cost guidance per model in the attached PDF. For predictable economics at scale we recommend a committed monthly token tier (see Ben's email below for discounts).

If you can confirm a 6- or 12-month commit range (or a target monthly token band), I can share a proposal with concrete discounted rates and the specific SLA for Dedicated capacity if you need it.

Best,
Aisha Rahman
Account Executive, Redwood Inference
aisha_rahman@redwood.ai
+1 (415) 555-0124
attachments: redwood_baseline_rates_v1.pdf

On Wed, May 8, 2029 at 09:12 AM Evan Strauss <evan@pebblehealth.ai> wrote:
> Hi Aisha,
> 
> We're evaluating hosted inference providers for a customer-facing summarization feature.... (see attached)


From: Ben Carter <ben_carter@redwood.ai>
To: Evan Strauss <evan@pebblehealth.ai>, Priya Malhotra <priya.malhotra@pebblehealth.ai>
Cc: Aisha Rahman <aisha_rahman@redwood.ai>
Date: Thu, 9 May 2029 10:22:00 -0700
Subject: RW proposal: baseline + commit discounts for PebbleHealth (RW-20290508-001)

Hi Evan / Priya — looping in to put some numbers on the table. Thanks Aisha for the intro and baseline.

We can offer the following committed monthly tiers (hosted API, per-1M token rates shown):
- No commit (public): input $0.45 / 1M, output $0.90 / 1M
- 6-month commit (>= 50M tokens/month): input $0.36 / 1M (20% off), output $0.72 / 1M (20% off)
- 12-month commit (>= 100M tokens/month): input $0.30 / 1M (33% off), output $0.60 / 1M (33% off)

Model availability on hosted remains the same across tiers; Dedicated capacity is an option if you need an SLA-backed latency target. We've attached a proposed commit-tier workbook that lays out example monthly invoices at your banded volumes.

Other commercial notes:
- We can include a one-time onboarding credit for pilots (ask Aisha for specifics).
- Standard terms are Redwood's MSP; we can accommodate customer PO and 30/45 day net terms for established customers.

Next steps: if you confirm a target starting monthly band (15M or 50M), I'll produce a short commercial summary and term sheet you can take to procurement.

Ben Carter
Commercial Lead, Redwood Inference
ben_carter@redwood.ai
attachments: RW-proposed-commit-tiers-2029-05-08.xlsx

Ben / Aisha — thanks for the quick turnaround and the workbook. A couple quick items:

- The 6-month commit band looks closest to where we may land after pilot. Can you confirm whether the 6-month discount applies retroactively to pilot usage if we convert within 30 days?
- Procurement will want a short summary of invoicing cadence and whether you accept a single PO that covers the first quarter.
- We also have a data-security questionnaire (SOC 2 + logs). Can you point me to the relevant security pack or contact to start that thread?

Assuming we can get a short procurement-ready summary, we'll route this internally and aim to decide on a pilot in the next 7–10 business days.

Appreciate the clarity — Evan

source.subject: Warmup gating, queue behavior, and scale-up acceptance — Osprey follow-ups
messages:
From: Claire Zhou <c.zhou@osprey-ai.com>
To: Aditya Rao <aditya.rao@redwoodinference.com>, Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwoodinference.com>
Cc: Osprey Ops <ops@osprey-ai.com>
Date: 2029-04-11T09:08:00-07:00
Subject: Follow-up: warm-pool gating + queueing behavior (post-demo)

Thanks for the demo earlier — quick recap from my side and a few open questions we promised to surface: 

Summary:
- Target latency SLO for our product pages: p95 <= 300ms for short prompts (<= 64 tokens), p95 <= 450ms for typical customer chat flows (avg 128 tokens).
- Peak concurrency expected during sale events: ~2.5k concurrent requests sustained for ~10 minutes, spiking to ~5k for ~60s.
- Streaming is used for chat UI (clients expect first-token < 120ms, subsequent token cadence steady).

Open questions:
1) Warm-pool sizing: you mentioned a warm pool that keeps KV caches ready — what warm instances/seconds do you recommend to hit the above p95 targets for both short and typical flows?
2) Scale-up path: how long to move from 0 spare capacity to fully-primed instances under our account tier? Is there an observable degradation window and expected queue build-up pattern?
3) Queueing and backpressure: if burst overshoots by 20%, what does the steady-state queue latency look like and when will requests be rejected vs queued?

Attachments: sample traces we captured during our internal load test (streaming_trace_sample_20290410.jsonl).

Next steps: please send a short ops-level runbook or bullets we can hand to our SREs so they can map their expectations.

—Claire
Claire Zhou
Head of Infrastructure
Osprey AI

From: Aditya Rao <aditya.rao@redwoodinference.com>
To: Claire Zhou <c.zhou@osprey-ai.com>
Cc: Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwoodinference.com>, Marissa Cole <marissa.cole@redwoodinference.com>
Date: 2029-04-11T10:12:00-07:00
Subject: Re: Follow-up: warm-pool gating + queueing behavior (post-demo)

Claire — thanks for the clear recap and the trace file. Good questions; below are concise answers and an action plan. I kept it operational so your SREs can enact quickly.

Quick answers (high-level):
- Warm-pool recommendation: start with N_warm = 8 medium GPU instances per region (we'll size by your model variant) with a 90s preheat window. That gives p95 headroom for short prompts; bump to 12 if you see heavy streaming priming penalties.
- Scale-up: cold -> fully primed in ~75–110s depending on model and KV-cache size; during that window you will see queuing described below.
- Queueing/backpressure: our system queues requests up to a per-account soft queue (default 2000 concurrent queued tokens). With your 5k 60s spike example, expect a brief queueing tail; we recommend a circuit-breaker at ~30s request age to fail fast for non-blocking UX.

Detailed ops bullets to hand to SREs (attachment: warmup_runbook_v1.pdf):
1) Warm-pool sizing rubric:
   - Small model (7B): 6–8 warm instances per region
   - Medium (13B): 8–12 warm instances per region
   - Large (>30B): 14+ warm instances or dedicated capacity
   - For your mix (50% short, 50% chat): start at 10 medium warm instances, monitor p95 over 24h, +25% if observed p95 > target.
2) Preheat window: 60–120s depending on KV cache size; default we use 90s.
3) Scale-up timeline and phases:
   - 0–20s: scheduler allocates containers and initializes kernels (CPU-bound)
   - 20–60s: model weights are loaded to GPU and KV cache warm starts
   - 60–110s: final priming, streaming handshake warmups complete
4) Expected queue behavior during spike:
   - For a 5k/60s spike with 2.5k baseline: short-term queue depth grows for ~30–90s, then drains as warm-pool becomes available. Tail p99 may exceed SLO by 2–3x during worst 30s.
   - If spike is sustained >3min, autoscaler will add replica sets and latency returns to baseline.
5) Streaming-specific notes:
   - First-token latency: warm instances meet <120ms; cold path can push first-token to 300–600ms.
   - For progressive UX we recommend client-side optimistic rendering with graceful rejoin on late first-token.

Action items (owner in parentheses):
- I will produce a trimmed runbook PDF for your SREs (Aditya) — attached.
- Ben to share an example Slack playbook and a sample circuit-breaker config we use with customers (Ben).
- Marissa to propose a short capacity add-on quote for Dedicated warm-pool sizing (Marissa).

Can you confirm the model family (7B/13B/30B) you plan to use in production and the percent streaming sessions vs batch inference? That will let us finalize warm-pool numbers.

Thanks — Aditya
Aditya Rao
Solutions Engineer, Redwood Inference

From: Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwoodinference.com>
To: Claire Zhou <c.zhou@osprey-ai.com>, Aditya Rao <aditya.rao@redwoodinference.com>
Cc: Marissa Cole <marissa.cole@redwoodinference.com>
Date: 2029-04-11T11:03:00-07:00
Subject: Re: Follow-up: warm-pool gating + queueing behavior (post-demo)

Adding a few operational examples from other customers to give you a feel for real behavior. Quick notes: 

Example A (retail flash-sale): 3k -> 6k spike for 90s, used a 10-instance warm-pool + 30s aggressive seeding. Results: median p95 stayed within target; p99 spiked for 20s during kernel init.

Example B (chat-first product): 1.2k baseline, 4k 60s spike, streaming-heavy: required client-side 2s optimistic window + 25% extra warm capacity to keep first-token under 150ms for >95% sessions.

Slack playbook snippet (paste-ready):
- Alert: "WarmPoolPressure > 80%" -> channel #ops-alerts + runbook link
- If queue depth > 1500 : increase warm-pool by 25% (manual toggle for first 2 events)
- If average first-token latency > 250ms for 5m: escalate to SRE oncall

I'll attach a short config example in Slack format to the shared Drive.

—Ben
Ben Carter
Senior Solutions Engineer, Redwood Inference

From: Claire Zhou <c.zhou@osprey-ai.com>
To: Aditya Rao <aditya.rao@redwoodinference.com>, Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwoodinference.com>, Marissa Cole <marissa.cole@redwoodinference.com>
Date: 2029-04-11T14:22:00-07:00
Subject: Re: Follow-up: warm-pool gating + queueing behavior (post-demo)

Thanks — this is exactly what we needed. Quick answers to your questions:
- Model family: predominantly 13B for production; we have a small % (10%) of 30B for high-fidelity flows.
- Streaming vs batch: ~70% streaming chat sessions, 30% embedding/batch calls.

Next steps from Osprey:
- We'll provision an initial warm-pool of 12 medium instances in us-west, as you suggested.
- Ops will run the attached trace against the warmup_runbook to validate queuing behavior and report p95/p99 to you by EOD Friday.
- We'll adopt the circuit-breaker suggestion at 30s and test UX fallback.

Could you also include the minimal telemetry keys we should send to your observability hooks so you can map warm-pool pressure and queue depth into the Redwood Console dashboards?

Claire
—Claire Zhou
Head of Infrastructure
Osprey AI

source.subject: Short window service patch — immediate verification requested
messages:
From: Aditya Rao <aditya@redwood.ai>
To: Ben Carter <ben@redwood.ai>, Laura Bennett <laura@redwood.ai>, Priyom Das <priyom@redwood.ai>
Date: 2028-11-14T08:12:00-08:00
Subject: Short window patch required for scheduler jitter (customer-facing note drafted)

Team —

We found a narrow but reproducible regression in the request scheduler that intermittently increases tail latency when clients send many concurrent short streaming requests (multi-token streaming bursts). Root cause: race in the slot reclamation path under high concurrent interleaving. Impact is limited but visible on a subset of customers (notably Acme Retail) as 200–400ms spikes on tail p50->p99 for short requests.

Planned immediate mitigation: a lightweight hotfix to force a deterministic slot release path and tighten the reclamation lock window. This is a short, in-process patch (no model reload) that we can deploy cluster-by-cluster with a 30–40 minute maintenance window. Expect transient reconnects for long-lived server-sent streams during the individual node restart; regular REST calls will get routed to healthy nodes and face at most a 1–2s retry.

Customer-facing note below (draft) — please review and sign off: we need Ben to approve the tone and Laura to prepare the verification checklist and post-patch logs link. Priyom, please own the rollout (canary -> region).

Attachment: patch runbook (hotfix_patch_20281114.diff) and verification_checklist.txt

Thanks,
Aditya
Director, Platform Reliability
Redwood Inference

---
Draft customer-facing message (internal):
Subject: Short maintenance window today — brief patch to improve streaming latency

Hi Sabrina —

We’re reaching out to let you know we’ve identified a short scheduler issue that can cause occasional spikes in streaming latency. We’re planning a short hotfix today (target start 09:30 PST) to address this. The fix is low-risk, will not change model versions, and we expect minimal disruption. You may see transient reconnects for long streaming sessions during node restarts; REST calls should continue to be served with automatic retries.

We’ll confirm completion and provide a brief checklist so you can verify your endpoints. If you’d prefer we schedule a different time, let me know immediately.

-Aditya
From: Aditya Rao <aditya@redwood.ai>
To: Sabrina Mills <sabrina@acmeretail.com>
Cc: Ben Carter <ben@redwood.ai>, Laura Bennett <laura@redwood.ai>
Date: 2028-11-14T08:45:00-08:00
Subject: Short maintenance window today — brief patch to improve streaming latency

Sabrina —

Thanks for the quick sync earlier this week. I wanted to give you a heads-up that we’re deploying a short hotfix for a scheduler race that can cause intermittent tail-latency spikes for streaming-heavy workloads.

Planned start: 09:30 PST
Estimated duration: 30–40 minutes (per cluster node, rolling)

title: Short window service patch — immediate verification requested
preview:
From: Aditya Rao <aditya@redwood.ai>
To: Ben Carter <ben@redwood.ai>, Laura Bennett <laura@redwood.ai>, Priyom Das <priyom@redwood.ai>
Date: 2028-11-14T08:12:00-08:00
Subject: Short window patch required for scheduler jitter (customer-facing note drafted)

Team —

We found a narrow but reproducible regression in the request scheduler that intermittently increases tail latency when clients send many concurrent short streaming requests (multi-token streaming bursts). Root cause: race in the slot reclamation path under high concurrent interleaving. Impact is limited but visible on a subset of customers (notably Acme Retail) as 200–400ms spikes on tail p50->p99 for short requests.

Planned immediate mitigation: a lightweight hotfix to force a deterministic slot release path and tighten the reclamation lock window. This is a short, in-process patch (no model reload) that we can deploy cluster-by-cluster with a 30–40 minute maintenance window. Expect transient reconnects for long-lived server-sent streams during the individual node restart; regular REST calls will get routed to healthy nodes and face at most a 1–2s retry.

Customer-facing note below (draft) — please review and sign off: we need Ben to approve the tone and Laura to prepare the verification checklist and post-patch logs link. Priyom, please own the rollout (canary -> region).

Attachment: patch runbook (hotfix_patch_20281114.diff) and verification_checklist.txt

Thanks,
Aditya
Director, Platform Reliability
Redwood Inference

source.subject: NOVAtech: sustained inference slowdowns after canary - timeline & ask
messages:
From: Sanjay Rao <sanjay@novatech.ai>
To: Alex Martinez <alex.martinez@redwood.com>
Date: Thu, 9 Nov 2028 15:12:00 -0800
Subject: NOVA - sudden increase in latency and errors since last canary

Alex —

We started seeing a significant degradation in inference performance across several endpoints shortly after your team flipped the canary yesterday afternoon. Our P95 jumped from ~110ms to ~480ms and P99s are consistently 1.4–1.9s. Error rates climbed from 0.2% to ~3% during heavy tails. This is impacting synchronous search flows; downstream timeouts have triggered customer complaints.

We rolled back our own client-side retries but the issue tracks to your hosted endpoints (region us-west2). The spike aligns with the canary timestamp: Nov 9 14:40 PST. Attached are a small sample of traces and our aggregated latency metrics.

We need an immediate mitigation plan and timeline for a rollback or other fix. SLA credits and customer communication will likely be required if this persists. Please advise next steps ASAP — our production traffic has retries and queuing that are beginning to cascade.

Regards,
Sanjay Rao
Head of Platform Engineering
NOVAtech

attachments: novatech_traces_sample.txt, novatech_latency_logs_20281109.zip

From: Alex Martinez <alex.martinez@redwood.com>
To: Sanjay Rao <sanjay@novatech.ai>
Cc: Marissa Cole <marissa.cole@redwood.com>, Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwood.com>
Date: Thu, 9 Nov 2028 15:28:00 -08:00
Subject: Re: NOVA - sudden increase in latency and errors since last canary

Sanjay — thanks for the quick signal and the traces, this is top priority. I'm escalating to SRE/serving and will coordinate a mitigation plan now.

Immediate actions we are starting:
- Marissa and team will validate whether the canary routing flag fully propagated and capture in-flight traffic (we'll begin a capture window now).
- If we see a regression tied to the variant, we'll flip routing back to the stable model within a 15–30 minute window.
- I will request a short SLA credit while we validate root cause; we'll confirm the amount after we quantify impacted qps.

Can you confirm: (1) whether you still see the same issue in us-east; (2) if you have a preferred rollback window (we can do immediate rollback but want to coordinate to avoid client-side flapping)?

I'll keep you updated every 15 mins until we stabilize.

Thanks — Alex
Alex Martinez | Account Executive, Redwood Inference
T: +1 415 555 0198

On Thu, Nov 9, 2028 at 3:12 PM Sanjay Rao <sanjay@novatech.ai> wrote:
> Alex —
> ... (original message)

From: Marissa Cole <marissa.cole@redwood.com>
To: Alex Martinez <alex.martinez@redwood.com>, Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwood.com>
Cc: Grace O'Connor <grace.oconnor@redwood.com>
Date: Thu, 9 Nov 2028 15:41:00 -08:00
Subject: Re: NOVA - sudden increase in latency and errors since last canary

Alex/Ben — I'm live on this. Quick summary from an SRE perspective after an initial pass on the captures:
- Canary variant exposes a slightly different attention kernel and has higher KV cache churn on longer prompt workloads. We see a ~22% slower per-token path when the model hits long context (800+ tokens).
- Routing shows ~12% of NOVA traffic was routed to the canary in us-west2 (matches their window).
- There is an interaction with our prefix caching: large short-lived prefixes caused cache eviction that increases cold-path QKV compute.

Immediate remediation options:
1) Toggle routing to stable variant for NOVA (model flag flip) — fastest, low-risk; will require ~5–10s propagation and minor extra qps on stable.
2) Apply a temporary routing weight reduction (e.g., 100% -> 5%) while we apply a targeted micro-patch to the canary kernel.
3) If they prefer, we can force a selective degrader that routes long-context requests to a smaller quantized fallback model.

I attached the canary-config-diff that highlights the kernel and kv-cache defaults. If Alex approves, I'll push route-toggle and monitor.

Marissa Cole
SRE — Serving Runtime
Redwood Inference

attachments: canary-config-diff.txt

From: Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwood.com>
To: Alex Martinez <alex.martinez@redwood.com>, Sanjay Rao <sanjay@novatech.ai>
Cc: Grace O'Connor <grace.oconnor@redwood.com>, Marissa Cole <marissa.cole@redwood.com>
Date: Thu, 9 Nov 2028 15:47:00 -08:00
Subject: Re: NOVA - sudden increase in latency and errors since last canary

Sanjay — appreciate the signal and the traces. NOVA is a high-value account and we want to minimize customer impact. Alex: please proceed with Marissa's recommended route-toggle to stable (option 1) and let's communicate a rollback window of immediate -> +15 mins to NOVA.

Grace — FYI, we will need to discuss a goodwill credit and a short customer note. I'm proposing a 7% usage credit for the affected window, pending exact qps analysis. If leadership wants to standardize the credit or cap it, let me know.

Alex — confirm and proceed; I'll draft the customer note and coordinate CS outreach.

— Ben
Account Executive, Redwood Inference

From: Alex Martinez <alex.martinez@redwood.com>
To: Jonas Weber <jonas.weber@redwood.com>, Grace O'Connor <grace.oconnor@redwood.com>, Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwood.com>, Marissa Cole <marissa.cole@redwood.com>
Date: Thu, 9 Nov 2028 16:05:00 -08:00
Subject: Exec brief: NOVA slowdown — immediate mitigation & asks

Jonas/Leadership — short brief. NOVA (DEAL-4478) reported a production degradation synchronous with our canary flip at 14:40 PST. Impact: P95 from ~110ms -> ~480ms; P99s up to ~1.9s; error rate delta ~+2.8% for a 20-minute window.

What we did (15:28–16:02 PST):
- Marissa identified a kernel + cache interaction in the canary variant.
- We toggled routing back to stable for NOVA at 15:48 PST. Propagation completed by 15:55 PST; metrics started trending back to baseline.
- We captured traces, config diffs and started a detailed postmortem. Attachments are in the incident link.

Asks:
- Approve a provisional 7% usage credit for the impacted window; we'll reconcile after exact qps math.
- Approve prioritization of a micro-patch on the canary kernel to address KV cache behavior (target: hotfix in 48 hours).
- Allow CS to send a brief customer-facing incident note (I will draft).

Status: NOVA confirmed they observe recovery after routing flip and requested a postmortem.

Alex Martinez
Account Executive — Strategic
Redwood Inference

On Thu, 9 Nov 2028 at 3:47 PM Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwood.com> wrote:
> Sanjay — appreciate the signal and the traces...

From: Sanjay Rao <sanjay@novatech.ai>
To: Alex Martinez <alex.martinez@redwood.com>, Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwood.com>
Cc: procurement@novatech.ai
Date: Fri, 10 Nov 2028 11:03:00 -08:00
Subject: Re: NOVA - sudden increase in latency and errors since last canary

Alex/Team — thank you for the quick action and for flipping routing back. We're seeing metrics return to expected ranges in us-west2 and I haven't received new customer complaints since this morning.

Please proceed with the postmortem and include: exact rollback timeline, root cause explanation, and the plan for preventing recurrence (including any model-board gating or rollout policy changes). We will coordinate on the customer note; procurement is copied for SLA/credits.

Sanjay

> On Nov 9, 2028, at 3:28 PM, Alex Martinez <alex.martinez@redwood.com> wrote:
> > Sanjay — thanks for the quick signal...

- Routing shows ~12% of NOVA traffic was routed to the canary in us-west2 (matches their window).
- There is an interaction with our prefix caching: large short-lived prefixes caused cache eviction that increases cold-path QKV compute.

Immediate remediation options:
1) Toggle routing to stable variant for NOVA (model flag flip) — fastest, low-risk; will require ~5–10s propagation and minor extra qps on stable.
2) Apply a temporary routing weight reduction (e.g., 100% -> 5%) while we apply a targeted micro-patch to the canary kernel.
3) If they prefer, we can force a selective degrader that routes long-context requests to a smaller quantized fallback model.

I attached the canary-config-diff that highlights the kernel and kv-cache defaults. If Alex approves, I'll push route-toggle and monitor.

Marissa Cole
SRE — Serving Runtime
Redwood Inference

attachments: canary-config-diff.txt

From: Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwood.com>
To: Alex Martinez <alex.martinez@redwood.com>, Sanjay Rao <sanjay@novatech.ai>
Cc: Grace O'Connor <grace.oconnor@redwood.com>, Marissa Cole <marissa.cole@redwood.com>
Date: Thu, 9 Nov 2028 15:47:00 -08:00
Subject: Re: NOVA - sudden increase in latency and errors since last canary

Sanjay — appreciate the signal and the traces. NOVA is a high-value account and we want to minimize customer impact. Alex: please proceed with Marissa's recommended route-toggle to stable (option 1) and let's communicate a rollback window of immediate -> +15 mins to NOVA.

Grace — FYI, we will need to discuss a goodwill credit and a short customer note. I'm proposing a 7% usage credit for the affected window, pending exact qps analysis. If leadership wants to standardize the credit or cap it, let me know.

Alex — confirm and proceed; I'll draft the customer note and coordinate CS outreach.

— Ben
Account Executive, Redwood Inference

From: Alex Martinez <alex.martinez@redwood.com>
To: Jonas Weber <jonas.weber@redwood.com>, Grace O'Connor <grace.oconnor@redwood.com>, Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwood.com>, Marissa Cole <marissa.cole@redwood.com>
Date: Thu, 9 Nov 2028 16:05:00 -08:00
Subject: Exec brief: NOVA slowdown — immediate mitigation & asks

Jonas/Leadership — short brief. NOVA (DEAL-4478) reported a production degradation synchronous with our canary flip at 14:40 PST. Impact: P95 from ~110ms -> ~480ms; P99s up to ~1.9s; error rate delta ~+2.8% for a 20-minute window.

What we did (15:28–16:02 PST):
- Marissa identified a kernel + cache interaction in the canary variant.
- We toggled routing back to stable for NOVA at 15:48 PST. Propagation completed by 15:55 PST; metrics started trending back to baseline.
- We captured traces, config diffs and started a detailed postmortem. Attachments are in the incident link.

Asks:
- Approve a provisional 7% usage credit for the impacted window; we'll reconcile after exact qps math.
- Approve prioritization of a micro-patch on the canary kernel to address KV cache behavior (target: hotfix in 48 hours).
- Allow CS to send a brief customer-facing incident note (I will draft).

Status: NOVA confirmed they observe recovery after routing flip and requested a postmortem.

Alex Martinez
Account Executive — Strategic
Redwood Inference

On Thu, 9 Nov 2028 at 3:47 PM Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwood.com> wrote:
> Sanjay — appreciate the signal and the traces...


From: Sanjay Rao <sanjay@novatech.ai>
To: Alex Martinez <alex.martinez@redwood.com>, Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwood.com>
Cc: procurement@novatech.ai
Date: Fri, 10 Nov 2028 11:03:00 -08:00
Subject: Re: NOVA - sudden increase in latency and errors since last canary

text:
From: Sanjay Rao <sanjay@novatech.ai>
To: Alex Martinez <alex.martinez@redwood.com>
Date: Thu, 9 Nov 2028 15:12:00 -0800
Subject: NOVA - sudden increase in latency and errors since last canary

Alex —

We started seeing a significant degradation in inference performance across several endpoints shortly after your team flipped the canary yesterday afternoon. Our P95 jumped from ~110ms to ~480ms and P99s are consistently 1.4–1.9s. Error rates climbed from 0.2% to ~3% during heavy tails. This is impacting synchronous search flows; downstream timeouts have triggered customer complaints.

We rolled back our own client-side retries but the issue tracks to your hosted endpoints (region us-west2). The spike aligns with the canary timestamp: Nov 9 14:40 PST. Attached are a small sample of traces and our aggregated latency metrics.

We need an immediate mitigation plan and timeline for a rollback or other fix. SLA credits and customer communication will likely be required if this persists. Please advise next steps ASAP — our production traffic has retries and queuing that are beginning to cascade.

Regards,
Sanjay Rao
Head of Platform Engineering
NOVAtech

attachments: novatech_traces_sample.txt, novatech_latency_logs_20281109.zip

- If we see a regression tied to the variant, we'll flip routing back to the stable model within a 15–30 minute window.
- I will request a short SLA credit while we validate root cause; we'll confirm the amount after we quantify impacted qps.

Can you confirm: (1) whether you still see the same issue in us-east; (2) if you have a preferred rollback window (we can do immediate rollback but want to coordinate to avoid client-side flapping)?

I'll keep you updated every 15 mins until we stabilize.

Thanks — Alex
Alex Martinez | Account Executive, Redwood Inference

source.subject: Request: Executive ratification for >20% volume rebate for Helix Therapeutics
messages:
From: Jordan Marks <jordan.marks@helixtx.com>
To: Aisha Rahman <aisha.rahman@redwood.com>
Cc: Procurement Team <procurement@helixtx.com>
Date: 2028-09-03T09:15:00-07:00
Subject: Hosted API volume commitment — pricing ask

Hi Aisha,

Thanks again for the demo yesterday — the latency profiles and cost breakdown were very helpful for our infra team. We looked at expected consumption for a 12-month pilot + phased production ramp and project ~2.5B tokens/month by Q2.

Given that scale, our procurement team is asking if Redwood can extend a 24% off list discount on Hosted API usage (applies to generation tokens only) in exchange for a 12-month committed spend and a joint announcement. We also need standard SOC2 evidence and a 90-day data-retention SLA in the contract.

I attached the SOW and our usage forecast. Can you route this for an executive pricing review? We need a yes/no before our fiscal close on Sep 12.

Appreciate it — Jordan

[Attachment: Helix_SOW_v1.pdf]
[Attachment: Helix_usage_forecast_Q4-2028.xlsx]

From: Aisha Rahman <aisha.rahman@redwood.com>
To: Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwood.com>, Marcus Lin <marcus.lin@redwood.com>
Cc: Markus Klein <markus.klein@redwood.com>
Date: 2028-09-03T11:02:00-07:00
Subject: Fwd: Hosted API volume commitment — pricing ask

Ben / Marcus — forwarding a large-volume ask from Helix. Summary: 12-month committed spend, projected 2.5B tokens/month, they want 24% off generation unit price + SOC2 + 90-day retention (they'll sign addendum). They’ve promised a joint announcement and product references.

Why I’m escalating: our standard delegated approval caps at 20% for Hosted API list prices. This is outside that band and would materially affect unit economics unless we lock in commitment and laddered pricing and tighten the rev share baseline.

Key asks for leadership:
- Approve one-time authorization for up to 24% off base generation rate conditioned on a minimum committed ARR (propose $4.2M ARR).
- If approved, require: 6-month performance review clause, automatic revert to baseline pricing if usage drops >40% vs forecast for two consecutive months, and no additional concessions (no free credits).

Attachments: their SOW and forecast attached. I recommend conditional approval to close — we’d need legal to add the SLA/data-retention language into the Master Services Agreement.

Aisha
Account Exec — Enterprise Sales
Redwood Inference

[Forwarded attachments retained]

On Sep 3, 2028 Jordan Marks <jordan.marks@helixtx.com> wrote:
> (original message)

From: Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwood.com>
To: Aisha Rahman <aisha.rahman@redwood.com>, Marcus Lin <marcus.lin@redwood.com>
Cc: Markus Klein <markus.klein@redwood.com>
Date: 2028-09-04T08:30:00-07:00
Subject: Re: Fwd: Hosted API volume commitment — pricing ask

Aisha — thanks for the clear write-up. I agree this should be allowed conditionally but we need guardrails. My notes: 
- Approve up to 24% off PROVIDED we secure a 12-month minimum committed ARR >= $4.2M and a non-refundable ramp deposit of 10% of year-one committed spend.
- Add a price protection clause: if their avg. usage falls below 60% of forecast for any two consecutive months, pricing resets to baseline for the following month and credits are clawed back pro rata.
- Legal to push for SOC2 evidence and limit SLA exposure — 90-day retention is acceptable if limited to sandbox/test data; production data retention should follow standard terms unless legal green-lights otherwise.

Ben
VP, Sales Operations
Redwood Inference

On Sep 3, 2028 Aisha Rahman wrote:
> (forwarded message)

From: Marcus Lin <marcus.lin@redwood.com>
To: Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwood.com>, Aisha Rahman <aisha.rahman@redwood.com>
Cc: Markus Klein <markus.klein@redwood.com>
Date: 2028-09-04T12:55:00-07:00
Subject: Re: Fwd: Hosted API volume commitment — pricing ask

Ben / Aisha — I support Ben’s conditional approval. I’ll authorize the deviation above our 20% delegation up to 24% so long as the following are included in the signed contract:
1) 12-month committed ARR >= $4.2M and a 10% ramp deposit (non-refundable).
2) Two-month performance review metric and automatic price reversion language (as Ben outlined).
3) A joint-PR must be approved by our comms team and cannot leak pricing details.

Markus, please have Legal draft the amendment to the MSA and restrict the data-retention SLA to the items Legal confirms (prefer sandbox-only for the 90-day ask unless otherwise signed off).

Marcus Lin
Chief Revenue Officer (on behalf of Sales Leadership)
Redwood Inference

On Sep 4, 2028 Ben Carter wrote:
> (approval thread)

From: Markus Klein <markus.klein@redwood.com>
To: Aisha Rahman <aisha.rahman@redwood.com>, Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwood.com>, Marcus Lin <marcus.lin@redwood.com>
Date: 2028-09-05T09:14:00-07:00
Subject: Re: Fwd: Hosted API volume commitment — pricing ask

Aisha — Legal has drafted an amendment. High level: we will permit a 24% discount on generation tokens only for 12 months, conditioned on a signed SOW that includes the 10% ramp deposit and the minimum committed ARR. Data-retention SLA will be limited to sandbox/test datasets; production retention follows standard MSA. I will circulate the redline (DocuSign-ready) by EOD. Please confirm you want me to include the joint-PR approval clause and the two-month performance-triggered reversion.

-Markus Klein
Sr. Counsel, Commercial
Redwood Inference

[Attachment: Proposed_Amendment_redline.docx]

From: Aisha Rahman <aisha.rahman@redwood.com>
To: Jordan Marks <jordan.marks@helixtx.com>
Cc: Procurement Team <procurement@helixtx.com>, Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwood.com>, Marcus Lin <marcus.lin@redwood.com>, Markus Klein <markus.klein@redwood.com>
Date: 2028-09-05T16:42:00-07:00
Subject: RE: Hosted API volume commitment — pricing ask

Jordan — good news. Leadership has approved a conditional 24% discount on generation usage for a 12-month term. Conditions:
- Minimum committed ARR: $4,200,000 for 12 months
- Non-refundable ramp deposit: 10% of year-one committed spend to be paid on signature
- Discount applies to generation tokens only; embeddings and other add-ons billed at standard rates
- Two-month performance review: if usage falls below 60% of forecast for two consecutive months, pricing reverts and any credits may be clawed back pro rata
- Data-retention: 90-day retention limited to sandbox/test data only; production data retention per MSA
- Joint announcement allowed but details need comms sign-off; pricing cannot be disclosed publicly

We’ve circulated a DocuSign-ready amendment (link below). Please review and sign by Sep 12 to meet your procurement deadline. Once signed, Legal will countersign and we’ll provision the commitment.

DocuSign: https://www.docusign.com/rec/HELIX-SOW/234567
HubSpot deal record: https://app.hubspot.com/deals/12345

Thanks — looking forward to getting Helix onboarded.

Best,
Aisha Rahman
Enterprise Account Executive
Redwood Inference

[Attachments retained: Helix_SOW_v1.pdf, Helix_usage_forecast_Q4-2028.xlsx]

- If approved, require: 6-month performance review clause, automatic revert to baseline pricing if usage drops >40% vs forecast for two consecutive months, and no additional concessions (no free credits).

Attachments: their SOW and forecast attached. I recommend conditional approval to close — we’d need legal to add the SLA/data-retention language into the Master Services Agreement.

Aisha
Account Exec — Enterprise Sales
Redwood Inference

[Forwarded attachments retained]

To: Aisha Rahman <aisha.rahman@redwood.com>, Marcus Lin <marcus.lin@redwood.com>
Cc: Markus Klein <markus.klein@redwood.com>

Subject: Re: Fwd: Hosted API volume commitment — pricing ask

- Add a price protection clause: if their avg. usage falls below 60% of forecast for any two consecutive months, pricing resets to baseline for the following month and credits are clawed back pro rata.
- Legal to push for SOC2 evidence and limit SLA exposure — 90-day retention is acceptable if limited to sandbox/test data; production data retention should follow standard terms unless legal green-lights otherwise.

Ben

1) 12-month committed ARR >= $4.2M and a 10% ramp deposit (non-refundable).
2) Two-month performance review metric and automatic price reversion language (as Ben outlined).
3) A joint-PR must be approved by our comms team and cannot leak pricing details.

Markus, please have Legal draft the amendment to the MSA and restrict the data-retention SLA to the items Legal confirms (prefer sandbox-only for the 90-day ask unless otherwise signed off).

source.subject: Re: Order Form / MSA — representations, warranty & indemnity sequencing
messages:
From: Laura Chen <laura.chen@optek.com>
To: Alex Martinez <alex.martinez@redwood.com>
Cc: Markus Becker <markus.becker@optek.com>
Date: 2028-07-29 09:15 -0700
Subject: Order Form: reps/warranty sequencing and indemnity questions

Alex — thanks again for the walkthrough this morning. Procurement pushed back on two items the legal team flagged: (1) the sequencing where customer representations are a condition to indemnity and (2) the evidence/operations handoff for third-party IP claims. Attached is our current redline (OPK-4321_OrderForm_v3_redlines.pdf).

Quick asks: can Redwood sign up to the sequencing language that prevents automatic denial of indemnity when representations are later found incorrect, provided customer cooperates and remedies within 30 days? And separately, we need clarity on who will own evidence collection (engineer contact) vs. litigation counsel — OpTek prefers a named liaison model in the MSA.

We have an internal deadline to present a revised MSA/Order Form to finance on Thurs EOD. Happy to jump on a 30-min call today.

Thanks,
Laura Chen
Head of Procurement — OpTek Systems

From: Alex Martinez <alex.martinez@redwood.com>
To: Laura Chen <laura.chen@optek.com>
Cc: Kimberly Park <kimberly.park@redwood.com>, Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwood.com>
Date: 2028-07-29 10:02 -0700
Subject: Re: Order Form: reps/warranty sequencing and indemnity questions

Laura — appreciate the redline. High-level response before we digest the PDF:

- Sequencing: Redwood is open to narrowing the sequencing so that a customer representation does not automatically void indemnity if (a) the customer cures or (b) Redwood had no reasonable notice and relied in good faith. We would want to preserve our right to decline indemnity where material misrepresentation was knowing or fraudulent.
- Evidence/liaison: we can name an operations liaison for evidence triage (SRE/Eng) and still reserve legal control for settlement decisions. Typical split: Redwood ops collects logs + model snapshots; Redwood counsel handles engagement with claimant counsel.

Kimberly / Ben — can you review Laura's redline and propose markups on the sequencing clause and the evidence-ownership matrix? If possible, I want a single-file redline back to OpTek by 5pm today so procurement can brief finance.

Alex Martinez
Head of GTM Partnerships — Redwood Inference

From: Kimberly Park <kimberly.park@redwood.com>
To: Alex Martinez <alex.martinez@redwood.com>
Cc: Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwood.com>
Date: 2028-07-29 13:45 -0700
Subject: Re: Order Form: reps/warranty sequencing and indemnity questions

Alex — reviewed Laura's attachment quickly and drafted two alternative inserts for sequencing: 

1) Cure-first sequencing (preferred by customer):
   "Notwithstanding any other provision, Customer's breach of a representation shall not relieve Redwood of its indemnification obligations if Customer cures the misrepresentation within thirty (30) days after receipt of Redwood's written notice and cooperates in the defense. For avoidance of doubt, the foregoing does not apply where Customer's misrepresentation was made knowingly or with fraudulent intent."

2) Risk-allocation sequencing (preferred by Redwood):
   "Redwood's obligation to indemnify shall be governed by the parties' respective obligations and the materiality of any representation. Where a representation is incorrect but the Customer promptly cooperates and remedies any issue which materially caused the claim, Redwood shall not be relieved of indemnity unless the misrepresentation was knowing or fraudulent or would reasonably be expected to have changed Redwood's decision to enter the Agreement."

On evidence ownership: I populated an "evidence-ownership-matrix.xlsx" that maps data types (request logs, session recordings, model artifacts, user prompts) to the owning party and retention windows. Main pushback: OpTek requested raw prompt logs be handed over on demand. We should push for redacted excerpts and a mutual evidence request process routed through legal (to avoid PII/PHI exposure).

I'll produce a clean redline and a short explainer paragraph for the procurement team.

Kimberly Park
Senior Counsel — Redwood Inference

From: Laura Chen <laura.chen@optek.com>
To: Alex Martinez <alex.martinez@redwood.com>
Cc: Markus Becker <markus.becker@optek.com>, Ellen Shaw <ellen.shaw@freemanfisherlaw.com>
Date: 2028-07-30 08:12 -0700
Subject: Re: Order Form: reps/warranty sequencing and indemnity questions

Thanks for the quick read-through, Kimberly. OpTek's outside counsel (Ellen) looped in to confirm they can accept the cure-first approach but want a slightly longer cure window (45 days) and a clause that preserves forensic preservation obligations during the cure period.

On prompts: procurement accepts redaction but says litigation may need full prompts for forensic triage. They ask Redwood to confirm an operational SLA for producing evidence (48–72 hours after legal request) and a secure transfer mechanism (SFTP or encrypted link).

Can Redwood confirm whether a 72-hour SLA for curated evidence (logs + model snapshot) is feasible? If not, please propose an alternative with interim milestones.

Laura

From: Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwood.com>
To: Laura Chen <laura.chen@optek.com>, Alex Martinez <alex.martinez@redwood.com>
Cc: Kimberly Park <kimberly.park@redwood.com>
Date: 2028-07-31 16:05 -0700
Subject: Re: Order Form: reps/warranty sequencing and indemnity questions

Laura / Alex — Ben here on Ops/Support. 72 hours for curated evidence is aggressive but achievable for prioritized incidents with a playbook. Proposed operational commitments: 

- T0: Legal request submitted via secure ticket (Doc: related_links)
- T+24h: Acknowledgement and identification of data owners; preliminary artifact export (logs index + retention manifest)
- T+72h: Delivery of curated evidence bundle (redacted prompts, request logs, relevant model snapshot). For production incidents requiring deeper retrieval, provide best-effort extended timeline and weekly updates.

We will not deliver raw customer prompt streams unless compelled by court order; instead, we will provide verified extracts and a notarized evidence manifest where required. We'll add this as language in the redline and include the evidence matrix. 

Ben Carter
Director, Customer Reliability — Redwood Inference
Attachment: evidence-ownership-matrix.xlsx

source.subject: Immediate Advisory: outbound peering disruption — recommended routing & audit steps
messages:
From: Alex Martinez <alex.martinez@redwood.com>
To: Javier Lugo <ops@acme.ai>, CloudPartner Integrations <partners@cloudpartner.com>
Cc: Monica Patel <monica.patel@redwood.com>, Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwood.com>
Date: 2028-07-21T09:12:00-07:00
Subject: Immediate Advisory: outbound peering disruption — recommended routing & audit steps

Hi Javier / team,

TL;DR: We are observing a targeted egress disruption affecting specific peering paths from our us-east and eu-west routing fabric to a small set of third-party cloud peers. Impact is visible as increased error rates and timeouts for requests that traverse those peers — primarily metadata and webhook egress used by some customers.

What we know so far:
- Start time: ~2028-07-21T08:40:00Z.
- Affected regions: us-east (primary) and eu-west (secondary).
- Symptoms: intermittent connection resets, 502/504 spikes for outbound calls to specific cloud peers; token generation and embeddings remain functional except for calls that rely on external webhook callbacks.
- Root cause (investigating): degraded egress peering on a small set of upstream providers. We have traffic telemetry and BGP tracebacks in progress with partners.

Immediate recommendations (what customers should do now):
1) If you control egress routing: apply the attached recommended-reroute-commands.txt to force outbound traffic over alternate peering (fallback-route-1) for the next 2–4 hours.
2) If you rely on webhooks: switch webhook endpoints to the provided relay endpoint (relay-acme.redwood.com) which tunnels outbound calls via an unaffected path.
3) For tenants on dedicated routing policies: opt into our temporary sidecar failover by replying with ACCEPT to this message — we will flip traffic and provide an audit log.

Operational notes and evidence we will provide:
- We will share an audit bundle with per-request traces and BGP tracebacks (attachment: audit-scope-ids.csv).
- A short connectivity diagram is attached showing the fallback topology.
- We will run a post-incident verification sweep and share a remediation schedule within 24 hours.

If you want Redwood to make the changes on your behalf (we can update routing for supported accounts), reply with the preferred contacts and a point of contact for validation.

Apologies for the disruption — we know even short egress interruptions are disruptive. I will keep you updated as we confirm the root cause with the upstream peers.

Attachments: connectivity-diagram.pdf, recommended-reroute-commands.txt, audit-scope-ids.csv

--
Alex Martinez
Senior Sales Engineer, Redwood Inference
alex.martinez@redwood.com | +1-415-555-0192
https://redwood.com
From: Javier Lugo <ops@acme.ai>
To: Alex Martinez <alex.martinez@redwood.com>
Cc: CloudPartner Integrations <partners@cloudpartner.com>
Date: 2028-07-21T09:37:00-07:00
Subject: Re: Immediate Advisory: outbound peering disruption — recommended routing & audit steps

Thanks Alex — quick questions:
1) Can you paste the exact iptables/nexthop commands we should run? Our infra team is on call and prefers copy/paste.
2) Does this affect model inference traffic (generation/embeddings) or just outbound webhooks/third-party integrations?
3) ETA for logs? We need them for our incident report.

We can give you ACCEPT to apply changes if you can include a short blast radius window and rollback steps.

On Jul 21, 2028, at 09:12, Alex Martinez wrote:
> TL;DR: We are observing a targeted egress disruption affecting specific peering paths from our us-east and eu-west routing fabric to a small set of third-party cloud peers.
>
> [attachment: recommended-reroute-commands.txt]

--
Javier Lugo
Head of Infrastructure, Acme AI
ops@acme.ai
From: Alex Martinez <alex.martinez@redwood.com>
To: Monica Patel <monica.patel@redwood.com>, Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwood.com>, Markus Klein <markus.klein@redwood.com>
Date: 2028-07-21T10:05:00-07:00
Subject: Fwd: Immediate Advisory: outbound peering disruption — internal actions

Forwarding external thread — immediate internal action items below.

=== Forwarded message ===
From: Alex Martinez <alex.martinez@redwood.com>
To: Javier Lugo <ops@acme.ai>
Date: 2028-07-21T09:12:00-07:00
Subject: Immediate Advisory: outbound peering disruption — recommended routing & audit steps

(see original advisory)

Internal action list (owners):
- Markus: validate the recommended-reroute-commands.txt on staging and produce a one-line rollback command. ETA 30m.
- Monica: prepare the audit bundle with per-request traces for all affected accounts (start by Acme AI). Put logs onto drive link and include HBSP-48392 in the bundle. ETA 90m.
- Ben: coordinate account approvals and confirm which accounts want Redwood to enact the change. Draft a short opt-in confirmation message we can send to customers. ETA 45m.
- Alex: keep customer comms and run the live validation call for Acme if they accept.

Notes: prioritize accounts with active webhooks first. We should also prepare a limited credit offer for impacted customers — Ben please align with finance if needed.

Attachments referenced: connectivity-diagram.pdf, recommended-reroute-commands.txt

--
Alex Martinez
From: Alex Martinez <alex.martinez@redwood.com>
To: Javier Lugo <ops@acme.ai>
Cc: CloudPartner Integrations <partners@cloudpartner.com>, Monica Patel <monica.patel@redwood.com>
Date: 2028-07-22T14:10:00-07:00
Subject: Resolved (partial): reroute confirmed; next steps for post-incident review

Hi Javier — quick update and next steps following your ACCEPT earlier today.

What we did:
- At 2028-07-21T10:20:00Z we flipped routes for Acme AI to fallback-route-1 (confirmed by traceroutes attached).
- 10:30–11:15Z: Monitoring showed request success rates returning to baseline for calls that previously failed over the affected peers.

Current status:
- Service: primary service and model inference are healthy; the impact was limited to outbound webhook/egress calls routed via the affected peers.

title: Immediate Advisory: outbound peering disruption — recommended routing & audit steps
preview:
From: Alex Martinez <alex.martinez@redwood.com>
To: Javier Lugo <ops@acme.ai>, CloudPartner Integrations <partners@cloudpartner.com>
Cc: Monica Patel <monica.patel@redwood.com>, Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwood.com>
Date: 2028-07-21T09:12:00-07:00
Subject: Immediate Advisory: outbound peering disruption — recommended routing & audit steps

Hi Javier / team,

TL;DR: We are observing a targeted egress disruption affecting specific peering paths from our us-east and eu-west routing fabric to a small set of third-party cloud peers. Impact is visible as increased error rates and timeouts for requests that traverse those peers — primarily metadata and webhook egress used by some customers.

What we know so far:
- Start time: ~2028-07-21T08:40:00Z.
- Affected regions: us-east (primary) and eu-west (secondary).
- Symptoms: intermittent connection resets, 502/504 spikes for outbound calls to specific cloud peers; token generation and embeddings remain functional except for calls that rely on external webhook callbacks.
- Root cause (investigating): degraded egress peering on a small set of upstream providers. We have traffic telemetry and BGP tracebacks in progress with partners.

Immediate recommendations (what customers should do now):
1) If you control egress routing: apply the attached recommended-reroute-commands.txt to force outbound traffic over alternate peering (fallback-route-1) for the next 2–4 hours.
2) If you rely on webhooks: switch webhook endpoints to the provided relay endpoint (relay-acme.redwood.com) which tunnels outbound calls via an unaffected path.
3) For tenants on dedicated routing policies: opt into our temporary sidecar failover by replying with ACCEPT to this message — we will flip traffic and provide an audit log.

Operational notes and evidence we will provide:
- We will share an audit bundle with per-request traces and BGP tracebacks (attachment: audit-scope-ids.csv).
- A short connectivity diagram is attached showing the fallback topology.
- We will run a post-incident verification sweep and share a remediation schedule within 24 hours.

If you want Redwood to make the changes on your behalf (we can update routing for supported accounts), reply with the preferred contacts and a point of contact for validation.

Apologies for the disruption — we know even short egress interruptions are disruptive. I will keep you updated as we confirm the root cause with the upstream peers.

Attachments: connectivity-diagram.pdf, recommended-reroute-commands.txt, audit-scope-ids.csv

--
Alex Martinez
Senior Sales Engineer, Redwood Inference
alex.martinez@redwood.com | +1-415-555-0192
https://redwood.com

source.subject: Hearthline hosting constraints & connectivity — post-call playback
messages:
From: Marissa Cole <marissa.cole@redwood.ai>
To: Ashley Grant <ashley.grant@hearthline.com>, Tom Morales <tom.morales@hearthline.com>
Cc: Monica Patel <monica.patel@redwood.ai>, Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwood.ai>
Date: Thu, 11 Jul 2028 16:12:00 +0000
Subject: Hearthline hosting constraints & connectivity — post-call playback

Thanks Ashley & Tom — great conversation this morning. Quick playback from my side and proposed next steps so we have a single source of truth.

High-level goals we captured
- Move proof-of-concept to a private deployment (VPC) to keep PHI in-customer network and meet residency requirements.
- Maintain sub-100ms token latency for interactive clinician workflows where possible; batch async ingestion for analytics.
- Strong auditability (retention of request/response metadata) and key management integration.

Key constraints / blockers you emphasized
1) Network model: deployment must be air-gapped from the public internet by default. Outbound egress allowed only through a controlled NAT via customer-managed bastion or private peering to the vendor (no direct internet calls from inference hosts).
2) Data residency: All persistent logs and model artifacts must remain within Hearthline's AWS account in us-east-1. No backups to 3rd-party storage outside the account.
3) SOC2 evidence: you asked for a short extract of our SOC2 control mapping for data-at-rest, encryption-in-transit, and audit logging.
4) Procurement timeline: security review complete by end of month to meet Q3 pilot kickoff.

Immediate asks / actions we own
- I will share a network diagram draft that maps our recommended VPC architecture: private subnets for inference nodes, a small outbound egress path via customer NAT, and an optional S3 VPC endpoint for model syncs. (See attachment: hearthline-reqs-network-diagram-v1.png)
- Monica will pull the SOC2 control extract and a short readout mapping to the points you raised (encryption, logging, key management).
- Ben will prepare a slimmed-down procurement checklist (required firewall rules, service account roles, and expected lead times).

Proposed next steps and timeline
- Today (Jul 11): Deliver network diagram + SOC2 extract (Marissa / Monica).
- Jul 13: Short technical Q&A with your infra lead (30–45 min) to validate peering/NAT approach — suggested times attached in separate calendar invite.
- Jul 20: Draft SOW outline and pilot acceptance criteria (Ben).

Is there anything I missed or any detail you want spelled out differently? If the network model above is off, flag the exact constraint and we’ll update the diagram.

Thanks again — excited to get this moving.

—
Marissa Cole
Senior Solutions Engineer
Redwood Inference
marissa.cole@redwood.ai
+1 (415) 555-0142

Attachment: hearthline-reqs-network-diagram-v1.png (PNG, 1.2 MB)

From: Ashley Grant <ashley.grant@hearthline.com>
To: Marissa Cole <marissa.cole@redwood.ai>
Cc: Tom Morales <tom.morales@hearthline.com>, Monica Patel <monica.patel@redwood.ai>, Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwood.ai>
Date: Fri, 12 Jul 2028 09:08:00 -0400
Subject: Re: Hearthline hosting constraints & connectivity — post-call playback

Marissa — thank you, this is a helpful summary. A few clarifications: 

- Air-gap: we don't require full air-gap for the pilot; rather we need the ability to operate in an isolated mode with egress only to agreed endpoints. In practice that means: no outbound calls to unknown hosts, and all egress must be via our NAT with an allowlist.

- Model updates: we need to ensure model weights are stored in our S3 bucket and that Redwood's sync process operates from the bucket via an assumed role — we cannot allow model artifacts to be copied to vendor-managed buckets.

- Logging: fine to keep request metadata in-memory for real-time ops, but any long-term logs must be shipped to our Kinesis stream in-region under our account. Retention policy will be 90 days for anything containing PII.

I attached a short redlines doc to the calendar invite (not attached here) — will bring Tom in on the infra call. Confirming Jul 13 availability: 09:30–10:15 ET works for us.

Ashley
VP, Engineering
Hearthline Medical
ashley.grant@hearthline.com

On Thu, Jul 11, 2028 at 16:12 Marissa Cole <marissa.cole@redwood.ai> wrote:
> Thanks Ashley & Tom — great conversation this morning. Quick playback from my side and proposed next steps so we have a single source of truth.
>
> [trimmed for inline reply]

From: Marissa Cole <marissa.cole@redwood.ai>
To: Ashley Grant <ashley.grant@hearthline.com>, Tom Morales <tom.morales@hearthline.com>
Cc: Monica Patel <monica.patel@redwood.ai>, Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwood.ai>
Date: Sat, 13 Jul 2028 10:22:00 +0000
Subject: Re: Hearthline hosting constraints & connectivity — post-call playback

Ashley — perfect, thanks for the clarifications. Quick notes inline and what we'll bring to the infra call tomorrow: 

- Egress model: understood — we'll design the deployment with no internet-facing egress from inference hosts. All egress will be routable through your NAT and we'll provide a minimal allowlist of endpoints (model heartbeat, license check, metrics ingestion) that can be disabled for production if you prefer.

- Model syncs: we will implement an S3-based deployment flow where Redwood's deployment runner assumes an IAM role in Hearthline's account, pulls the model weights, and streams them directly to the inference nodes in the VPC. No copies to vendor-managed buckets. We'll capture the assumed role policy for your security review.

- Logs & retention: we can push structured operational logs to your Kinesis stream via an internal forwarder. We'll respect the 90-day retention and provide a separate write-up on PII scrubbing options before any persistent storage.

Attachments included: redwood-soc2-extract.pdf — control mapping (encryption in transit, KMS integration, audit logging).

Tomorrow's prep (Jul 13, 09:30–10:15 ET):
- Monica will run through the SOC2 mapping and KMS options (10–12 min)
- I'll demo the proposed VPC diagram and deployment flow (10–12 min)
- Ben will outline procurement/network rule asks and expected lead times (5–10 min)

If that timing still works, I'll send the calendar invite now.

—
Marissa

Attachment: redwood-soc2-extract.pdf (PDF, 240 KB)

From: Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwood.ai>
To: Ashley Grant <ashley.grant@hearthline.com>, Tom Morales <tom.morales@hearthline.com>
Cc: Marissa Cole <marissa.cole@redwood.ai>, Monica Patel <monica.patel@redwood.ai>
Date: Sat, 13 Jul 2028 22:02:00 +0000
Subject: Re: Hearthline hosting constraints & connectivity — post-call playback

Ashley / Tom — thanks again for the call. A couple operational notes from procurement/op side so you have clarity before the infra sync: 

- Firewall rules: we'll provide a minimal ruleset. For the pilot you will need to allow outbound to the following host/IPs (these are our control plane egress endpoints) — we'll keep them static for the pilot: 52.23.114.7, 52.23.114.8 (US-East). If you will only allow DNS names, we can provide stable hostnames.

- Service accounts & roles: expected minimum is one IAM role for model-sync with S3 read access, and one role allowing write-only to your Kinesis stream for logs. We'll share a template CloudFormation/IaC snippet after the call.

- Timeline & gating: with security sign-off (SOC2 mapping + assumed-role review) we can stand up the pilot environment in ~2 weeks. If you need a full air-gapped, no-allowlist production variant, that adds custom engineering (estimate +4–6 weeks) — happy to scope that separately.

Next steps I committed to:
- Share IaC template + firewall rule list (within 24 hours).
- Draft pilot SOW & acceptance criteria (by Jul 20).

I'll drop the IaC snippet into the Drive folder and flag HS-345672 in your HubSpot record.

Ben Carter
Head of Dedicated Deployments
Redwood Inference
ben.carter@redwood.ai

[internal note: Monica — if you can surface the KMS mapping, I’ll include it in the SOW draft]

Subject: Re: Hearthline hosting constraints & connectivity — post-call playback

Ashley / Tom — thanks again for the call. A couple operational notes from procurement/op side so you have clarity before the infra sync: 

- Firewall rules: we'll provide a minimal ruleset. For the pilot you will need to allow outbound to the following host/IPs (these are our control plane egress endpoints) — we'll keep them static for the pilot: 52.23.114.7, 52.23.114.8 (US-East). If you will only allow DNS names, we can provide stable hostnames.

- Service accounts & roles: expected minimum is one IAM role for model-sync with S3 read access, and one role allowing write-only to your Kinesis stream for logs. We'll share a template CloudFormation/IaC snippet after the call.

- Timeline & gating: with security sign-off (SOC2 mapping + assumed-role review) we can stand up the pilot environment in ~2 weeks. If you need a full air-gapped, no-allowlist production variant, that adds custom engineering (estimate +4–6 weeks) — happy to scope that separately.

Next steps I committed to:
- Share IaC template + firewall rule list (within 24 hours).
- Draft pilot SOW & acceptance criteria (by Jul 20).

I'll drop the IaC snippet into the Drive folder and flag HS-345672 in your HubSpot record.

Ben Carter
Head of Dedicated Deployments
Redwood Inference
ben.carter@redwood.ai

[internal note: Monica — if you can surface the KMS mapping, I’ll include it in the SOW draft]
text:
From: Marissa Cole <marissa.cole@redwood.ai>
To: Ashley Grant <ashley.grant@hearthline.com>, Tom Morales <tom.morales@hearthline.com>
Cc: Monica Patel <monica.patel@redwood.ai>, Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwood.ai>
Date: Thu, 11 Jul 2028 16:12:00 +0000
Subject: Hearthline hosting constraints & connectivity — post-call playback

Thanks Ashley & Tom — great conversation this morning. Quick playback from my side and proposed next steps so we have a single source of truth.

High-level goals we captured
- Move proof-of-concept to a private deployment (VPC) to keep PHI in-customer network and meet residency requirements.
- Maintain sub-100ms token latency for interactive clinician workflows where possible; batch async ingestion for analytics.
- Strong auditability (retention of request/response metadata) and key management integration.

Key constraints / blockers you emphasized
1) Network model: deployment must be air-gapped from the public internet by default. Outbound egress allowed only through a controlled NAT via customer-managed bastion or private peering to the vendor (no direct internet calls from inference hosts).
2) Data residency: All persistent logs and model artifacts must remain within Hearthline's AWS account in us-east-1. No backups to 3rd-party storage outside the account.
3) SOC2 evidence: you asked for a short extract of our SOC2 control mapping for data-at-rest, encryption-in-transit, and audit logging.
4) Procurement timeline: security review complete by end of month to meet Q3 pilot kickoff.

Immediate asks / actions we own
- I will share a network diagram draft that maps our recommended VPC architecture: private subnets for inference nodes, a small outbound egress path via customer NAT, and an optional S3 VPC endpoint for model syncs. (See attachment: hearthline-reqs-network-diagram-v1.png)
- Monica will pull the SOC2 control extract and a short readout mapping to the points you raised (encryption, logging, key management).
- Ben will prepare a slimmed-down procurement checklist (required firewall rules, service account roles, and expected lead times).

Proposed next steps and timeline
- Today (Jul 11): Deliver network diagram + SOC2 extract (Marissa / Monica).
- Jul 13: Short technical Q&A with your infra lead (30–45 min) to validate peering/NAT approach — suggested times attached in separate calendar invite.
- Jul 20: Draft SOW outline and pilot acceptance criteria (Ben).

Is there anything I missed or any detail you want spelled out differently? If the network model above is off, flag the exact constraint and we’ll update the diagram.

Thanks again — excited to get this moving.

—
Marissa Cole
Senior Solutions Engineer
Redwood Inference
marissa.cole@redwood.ai
+1 (415) 555-0142

Attachment: hearthline-reqs-network-diagram-v1.png (PNG, 1.2 MB)


From: Ashley Grant <ashley.grant@hearthline.com>
To: Marissa Cole <marissa.cole@redwood.ai>
Cc: Tom Morales <tom.morales@hearthline.com>, Monica Patel <monica.patel@redwood.ai>, Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwood.ai>
Date: Fri, 12 Jul 2028 09:08:00 -0400
Subject: Re: Hearthline hosting constraints & connectivity — post-call playback

Marissa — thank you, this is a helpful summary. A few clarifications: 

- Air-gap: we don't require full air-gap for the pilot; rather we need the ability to operate in an isolated mode with egress only to agreed endpoints. In practice that means: no outbound calls to unknown hosts, and all egress must be via our NAT with an allowlist.

- Model updates: we need to ensure model weights are stored in our S3 bucket and that Redwood's sync process operates from the bucket via an assumed role — we cannot allow model artifacts to be copied to vendor-managed buckets.

- Logging: fine to keep request metadata in-memory for real-time ops, but any long-term logs must be shipped to our Kinesis stream in-region under our account. Retention policy will be 90 days for anything containing PII.

I attached a short redlines doc to the calendar invite (not attached here) — will bring Tom in on the infra call. Confirming Jul 13 availability: 09:30–10:15 ET works for us.

Ashley
VP, Engineering
Hearthline Medical
ashley.grant@hearthline.com

On Thu, Jul 11, 2028 at 16:12 Marissa Cole <marissa.cole@redwood.ai> wrote:
> Thanks Ashley & Tom — great conversation this morning. Quick playback from my side and proposed next steps so we have a single source of truth.
>
> [trimmed for inline reply]

From: Marissa Cole <marissa.cole@redwood.ai>
To: Ashley Grant <ashley.grant@hearthline.com>, Tom Morales <tom.morales@hearthline.com>
Cc: Monica Patel <monica.patel@redwood.ai>, Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwood.ai>
Date: Sat, 13 Jul 2028 10:22:00 +0000
Subject: Re: Hearthline hosting constraints & connectivity — post-call playback

Ashley — perfect, thanks for the clarifications. Quick notes inline and what we'll bring to the infra call tomorrow: 

- Egress model: understood — we'll design the deployment with no internet-facing egress from inference hosts. All egress will be routable through your NAT and we'll provide a minimal allowlist of endpoints (model heartbeat, license check, metrics ingestion) that can be disabled for production if you prefer.

- Model syncs: we will implement an S3-based deployment flow where Redwood's deployment runner assumes an IAM role in Hearthline's account, pulls the model weights, and streams them directly to the inference nodes in the VPC. No copies to vendor-managed buckets. We'll capture the assumed role policy for your security review.

- Logs & retention: we can push structured operational logs to your Kinesis stream via an internal forwarder. We'll respect the 90-day retention and provide a separate write-up on PII scrubbing options before any persistent storage.

Attachments included: redwood-soc2-extract.pdf — control mapping (encryption in transit, KMS integration, audit logging).

Tomorrow's prep (Jul 13, 09:30–10:15 ET):
- Monica will run through the SOC2 mapping and KMS options (10–12 min)
- I'll demo the proposed VPC diagram and deployment flow (10–12 min)
- Ben will outline procurement/network rule asks and expected lead times (5–10 min)

If that timing still works, I'll send the calendar invite now.

—
Marissa

Attachment: redwood-soc2-extract.pdf (PDF, 240 KB)
