source.subject: Post-demo: dedicated lane pricing workshop + token/throughput scenarios
messages:
From: Aditya Rao <aditya@redwood.ai>
To: Sam Cho <sam.cho@crestlinehealth.com>
Cc: Marissa Cole <marissa@redwood.ai>
Date: Wed, 8 Sep 2027 15:02:00 -0700
Subject: Post-demo recap + next steps on dedicated lane pricing

Hey Sam — great to meet you and the Crestline team on the demo earlier. Quick recap and proposed next steps from our side: 

1) Goals we heard: ~1.2M monthly inference tokens initially, scaling to ~6–8M over 9–12 months; strict P50 latency target ~120ms for chat paths; PHI-sensitive content, so private deployment or dedicated lanes with VPC is a preference.

2) Immediate ask from you: an estimate of committed-reserved lane pricing for a 6-month pilot (reserved) and options for H100 vs A100 hardware given the latency/throughput targets. You also mentioned wanting a ballpark $/1M tokens target to compare vs your internal budget.

3) What we’ll deliver by EOD Friday: attached short pricing deck plus a simple cost model Excel with three scenarios (pilot 1.2M, growth 3.5M, scale 7.5M tokens) and per-1M token unit economics for A100 vs H100 reserved lanes.

Questions / asks for you: can you confirm the expected average prompt+generation length (tokens) for the chat flow (ballpark)? Also, who should we loop in on procurement/legal for NDA/PO path — procurement@crestline?

Attachments: redwood-dedicated-pricing-deck-v2.pdf (3 slides), crestline-cost-model.xlsx (scenario tabs).

Thanks — we can also run a 60–90 minute pricing workshop next week if you want to walk the model together.

— Aditya Rao
Account Executive | Redwood Inference
aditya@redwood.ai | +1 415-555-0123

From: Sam Cho <sam.cho@crestlinehealth.com>
To: Aditya Rao <aditya@redwood.ai>
Date: Thu, 9 Sep 2027 09:14:22 -0700
Subject: Re: Post-demo recap + next steps on dedicated lane pricing

Thanks Aditya — appreciate the quick follow. Couple clarifications and constraints from our side: 

- Token profile: average request is ~40 tokens prompt + ~220 tokens generation (so ~260 tokens total) for RnR/chat flows. Some short lookups are ~30 tokens total.
- Target throughput: for the pilot we expect bursty traffic with peaks ~45 concurrent sessions; long-term target throughput ~250 concurrent sessions (not all at once; that's cloth-peak over a region) — you can map to tokens/sec if easier.
- Budget guardrails: our procurement sponsor gave an initial guideline of $600–$1,200 / 1M tokens as acceptable band for a reserved pilot evaluation, depending on SLA and data isolation. Lower is better but we'll trade up for dedicated private options.
- Preferred hardware: open to either A100 or H100 but would like you to recommend based on latency and per-1M token economics.

Procurement contact is procurement@crestlinehealth.com — loop them in for contractual path. We’re aiming to start a pilot in mid-Oct if numbers align.

Sam
Senior Product Manager, Crestline Health
From: Aditya Rao <aditya@redwood.ai>
To: Sam Cho <sam.cho@crestlinehealth.com>
Cc: Marissa Cole <marissa@redwood.ai>, Ben Carter <ben@redwood.ai>
Date: Thu, 9 Sep 2027 12:46:10 -0700
Subject: Re: Post-demo recap + next steps on dedicated lane pricing

Thanks Sam — this is super helpful. Quick, high-fidelity response based on your token profile and the budget band you shared. I put the full math in the attached Excel but highlights below: 

Scenario assumptions (using your ~260 tokens/request):
- Pilot: 1.2M tokens/month (roughly ~4.6k requests/month at 260 tokens each)
- Growth: 3.5M tokens/month
- Scale: 7.5M tokens/month

Pricing highlights (reserved dedicated lanes, 6-month commit example):
- A100 lane (single-reserved lane sizing to meet ~120ms P50 for chat): estimated unit = ~$950 / 1M tokens (pilot band), drops to ~$720 / 1M at 3.5M, ~$610 / 1M at 7.5M due to utilization and batching efficiencies.
- H100 lane (preferred if you need headroom for lower latency & higher concurrency): estimated unit = ~$1,150 / 1M (pilot), ~$880 / 1M (3.5M), ~$720 / 1M (7.5M). H100 shows ~18–25% better latency/CPS on our benchmarks for this architecture but has a higher reserved cost.

Where the delta comes from: H100 gives higher throughput per reserved lane (so fewer lanes to buy at scale) but a higher absolute per-hour reserved cost; for small pilots A100 can be cheaper per token because you pay for less idle headroom. We included an intermediate quantized variant and a hybrid route (A100 reserve + H100 burst pool) in the workbook.

Operational notes:
- We can configure automatic fallback routing to a lower-cost quantized variant if utilization spikes and you want to preserve budget.
- Networking: for PHI-sensitivity we recommend Redwood Private (VPC) or Dedicated with customer-managed peering. Procurement impacts pricing; we flagged a compliance add-on in the workbook.

Next steps I propose:
1) Workshop (45–60m) to walk the cost model with procurement + engineering — suggested slots: Tue 14 Sep 10:00am PT or Wed 15 Sep 1:00pm PT.
2) If workshop aligns, we’ll produce a 1-page pilot SOW + PO path and provide a test account for a 2-week performance verification run.

Can you confirm which slot works and whether procurement should join the workshop? If yes, I’ll draft a short invite and share an updated workbook with hybrid-reserve scenarios.

— Aditya
From: Marissa Cole <marissa@redwood.ai>
To: Aditya Rao <aditya@redwood.ai>, Ben Carter <ben@redwood.ai>
Date: Fri, 10 Sep 2027 10:05:00 -0700
Subject: Internal: Crestline follow-up — prep items for pricing workshop

Aditya / Ben — quick internal note to capture next steps after Sam’s confirmation: 

1) Aditya to book Tue 14 Sep 10:00am PT workshop (pending client confirmation). Aditya will invite Sam + procurement@crestlinehealth.com + their lead infra engineer (TBD).
2) Ben — can you prepare two SKU options in the workbook: (a) 6-month reserved A100 lane pricing with per-1M token lines, (b) hybrid A100 reserved + H100 burst lane with a note on expected latency delta. Include a short column showing per-1M token at pilot, growth, scale volumes.
3) Marissa — I’ll draft the 1-page pilot SOW after the workshop; SalesOps should prepare a PO template and list any compliance add-on pricing we referenced.
4) Upload final workbook and deck to the Drive folder and share with procurement.

I’ve attached the current deck & workbook that Aditya shared. We should aim to keep the pilot list price within the $600–$1,200 / 1M band Sam mentioned; aim to demonstrate a clear path to sub-$700 / 1M at scale.

— Marissa
Head of Sales Strategy | Redwood Inference

- A100 lane (single-reserved lane sizing to meet ~120ms P50 for chat): estimated unit = ~$950 / 1M tokens (pilot band), drops to ~$720 / 1M at 3.5M, ~$610 / 1M at 7.5M due to utilization and batching efficiencies.
- H100 lane (preferred if you need headroom for lower latency & higher concurrency): estimated unit = ~$1,150 / 1M (pilot), ~$880 / 1M (3.5M), ~$720 / 1M (7.5M). H100 shows ~18–25% better latency/CPS on our benchmarks for this architecture but has a higher reserved cost.

Where the delta comes from: H100 gives higher throughput per reserved lane (so fewer lanes to buy at scale) but a higher absolute per-hour reserved cost; for small pilots A100 can be cheaper per token because you pay for less idle headroom. We included an intermediate quantized variant and a hybrid route (A100 reserve + H100 burst pool) in the workbook.

Operational notes:
- We can configure automatic fallback routing to a lower-cost quantized variant if utilization spikes and you want to preserve budget.
- Networking: for PHI-sensitivity we recommend Redwood Private (VPC) or Dedicated with customer-managed peering. Procurement impacts pricing; we flagged a compliance add-on in the workbook.

Next steps I propose:
1) Workshop (45–60m) to walk the cost model with procurement + engineering — suggested slots: Tue 14 Sep 10:00am PT or Wed 15 Sep 1:00pm PT.
2) If workshop aligns, we’ll produce a 1-page pilot SOW + PO path and provide a test account for a 2-week performance verification run.

Can you confirm which slot works and whether procurement should join the workshop? If yes, I’ll draft a short invite and share an updated workbook with hybrid-reserve scenarios.

— Aditya


From: Marissa Cole <marissa@redwood.ai>
To: Aditya Rao <aditya@redwood.ai>, Ben Carter <ben@redwood.ai>
Date: Fri, 10 Sep 2027 10:05:00 -0700
Subject: Internal: Crestline follow-up — prep items for pricing workshop

Aditya / Ben — quick internal note to capture next steps after Sam’s confirmation: 

1) Aditya to book Tue 14 Sep 10:00am PT workshop (pending client confirmation). Aditya will invite Sam + procurement@crestlinehealth.com + their lead infra engineer (TBD).
2) Ben — can you prepare two SKU options in the workbook: (a) 6-month reserved A100 lane pricing with per-1M token lines, (b) hybrid A100 reserved + H100 burst lane with a note on expected latency delta. Include a short column showing per-1M token at pilot, growth, scale volumes.
3) Marissa — I’ll draft the 1-page pilot SOW after the workshop; SalesOps should prepare a PO template and list any compliance add-on pricing we referenced.
4) Upload final workbook and deck to the Drive folder and share with procurement.

I’ve attached the current deck & workbook that Aditya shared. We should aim to keep the pilot list price within the $600–$1,200 / 1M band Sam mentioned; aim to demonstrate a clear path to sub-$700 / 1M at scale.

— Marissa
Head of Sales Strategy | Redwood Inference

source.subject: Multi-factor latency attribution questionnaire — instrumentation & sampling
messages:
From: Diane Huang <diane.huang@stratushealth.com>
To: Aditya Rao <aditya@redwood.com>
Cc: Alex Moreno <alex.moreno@stratushealth.com>
Date: Mon, 6 Sep 2027 09:12:00 -0700
Subject: RFI: detailed latency/throughput instrumentation questions

Hi Aditya,

Thanks for the call earlier. As we finalize the SOW for the evaluation, our procurement and infra teams asked for a short, focused questionnaire on how Redwood collects and attributes latency during endpoint benchmarking. Specifically we're trying to understand how to compare your runtime results to our in-house harness — we need:

- Which hop-level timestamps you capture (client, ingress LB, serving runtime, model execution, egress) and whether they are exposed per-request.
- How you sample and aggregate tail quantiles (p95/p99/p99.9) — do you rely on streaming telemetry or batch logs?
- How batching and KV cache hits are instrumented in output metrics.
- Guidance we can use to design an apples-to-apples test harness (what config knobs to control to avoid double-counting network vs token compute).

Can you provide the questionnaire as a short email or attachment we can include in the RFP? We expect to share this with our SRE team and would appreciate explicit examples of the headers/fields we should capture in the harness (timestamps, request ids, kvcache flags).

Thanks,
Diane
Diane Huang
Senior Procurement Manager
StratusHealth

Attachment: none in original message

From: Aditya Rao <aditya@redwood.com>
To: Diane Huang <diane.huang@stratushealth.com>
Cc: Alex Moreno <alex.moreno@stratushealth.com>, Monica Patel <monica@redwood.com>, Ben Carter <ben@redwood.com>
Date: Mon, 6 Sep 2027 11:08:00 -07:00
Subject: Re: RFI: detailed latency/throughput instrumentation questions

Diane — thanks for the note and the clarity. I put together a compact questionnaire and a short guidance runbook for your SREs. High level first: our goal is to separate transport/network latency from token-serving latency and from internal scheduling/queueing so you can compare numbers across harnesses.

Summary of what we capture (short):
- Client request timestamp (t_client_send) — at your harness client.
- Ingress receive timestamp (t_ingress_recv) — measured at the edge load balancer / API gateway.
- Runtime receive/start timestamp (t_runtime_start) — when the runtime dequeues and begins processing (after batching decision).
- Model inference start/end (t_model_start, t_model_end) — kernel / device-level timestamps where available.
- Egress send timestamp (t_runtime_send) and client receive (t_client_recv).
- KV cache indicators: per-request boolean flag and cache_hit_count.
- Batch size and position: numeric batch_size, ordinal position in batch (0-indexed).
- Fallback indicator: which model variant served the request.

Aggregation & sampling notes (short):
- We recommend streaming per-request traces to a time-series store (e.g., OTLP -> ClickHouse) with a stable request_id for joinability.
- Calculate quantiles on post-processed per-request compute-only latency (t_model_end - t_model_start) and include network-only (t_ingress_recv - t_client_send) as a separate series.
- For tail quantiles use sliding-window rolling aggregation (1h) to reduce transient noise during surges.
- For p99.9 consider reservoir sampling for very high QPS to bound storage while preserving tail fidelity.

I've attached a compact .xlsx with the exact column names we export (one row per request) and a 2-page runbook with an example harness config. Monica and Ben are CC'd to help with follow-ups and any data samples you want us to annotate.

Deliverables attached: rfi_instrumentation_template.xlsx, runbook-sampling.pdf, example-harness-config.yaml

Best,
Aditya Rao
Head of Solutions — Redwood Inference
aditya@redwood.com

---
On Mon, Sep 6, 2027 at 09:12, Diane Huang <diane.huang@stratushealth.com> wrote:
> Hi Aditya,
>
> Thanks for the call earlier. As we finalize the SOW for the evaluation, our procurement and infra teams asked for a short, focused questionnaire on how Redwood collects and attributes latency during endpoint benchmarking. Specifically we're trying to understand how to compare your runtime results to our in-house harness — we need:
>
> - Which hop-level timestamps you capture (client, ingress LB, serving runtime, model execution, egress) and whether they are exposed per-request.
> - How you sample and aggregate tail quantiles (p95/p99/p99.9) — do you rely on streaming telemetry or batch logs?
> - How batching and KV cache hits are instrumented in output metrics.
> - Guidance we can use to design an apples-to-apples test harness (what config knobs to control to avoid double-counting network vs token compute).
>
> Can you provide the questionnaire as a short email or attachment we can include in the RFP? We expect to share this with our SRE team and would appreciate explicit examples of the headers/fields we should capture in the harness (timestamps, request ids, kvcache flags).
>
> Thanks,
> Diane
>

From: Alex Moreno <alex.moreno@stratushealth.com>
To: Aditya Rao <aditya@redwood.com>
Cc: Diane Huang <diane.huang@stratushealth.com>, Monica Patel <monica@redwood.com>, Ben Carter <ben@redwood.com>, Kimberly Park <kimberly@redwood.com>
Date: Tue, 7 Sep 2027 08:35:00 -07:00
Subject: Follow-up question: workload shaping and synthetic tokenization

Aditya / team,

Thanks for the materials — we pulled the template into our harness. Two quick clarifications from our infra team before we instrument:

1) Workload shaping: when you run your internal "gradual ramp" tests, do you include synthetic long-sequence probes to exercise model attention/kv-cache (e.g., 4% of requests are 4k-token synthetic prompts)? If so, how do you mark/segregate those from live short requests in the datasets?

2) Tokenization parity: our tokenizer maps slightly different token counts to the open model variant. Do you recommend normalizing by token-count (compute-per-token) or by request wall time for cross-harness comparison?

Also we noticed you export a "batch_position" field — is that ordinal guaranteed to reflect final batch execution ordering? Our harness uses interleaving and it’s important we attribute per-token time reasonably.

Appreciate a quick pointer so we can finish instrumenting and run an initial comparison next week.

— Alex

From: Aditya Rao <aditya@redwood.com>
To: Alex Moreno <alex.moreno@stratushealth.com>, Diane Huang <diane.huang@stratushealth.com>
Cc: Monica Patel <monica@redwood.com>, Ben Carter <ben@redwood.com>, Kimberly Park <kimberly@redwood.com>
Date: Thu, 9 Sep 2027 15:42:00 -07:00
Subject: Re: Follow-up question: workload shaping and synthetic tokenization

Alex — quick answers below, then a short recommended practice you can paste into the RFP.

1) Workload shaping: yes — our internal surge runs include a small proportion (2–5%) of long synthetic sequences to exercise KV cache warmup and attention scaling. We tag those with an explicit synthetic_probe boolean and a synthetic_profile id so they can be excluded from production quantiles or used to produce a separate "long-tail" latency series. We recommend you do the same so you can produce two comparable views: normal traffic + synthetic-probes.

2) Tokenization parity: normalize where possible by effective token-count when reporting compute-per-token metrics (compute_ms_per_token = (t_model_end - t_model_start) / token_count). For end-to-end latency comparisons, report both compute-per-token and wall-time percentiles. That lets you see whether tokenizer differences are causing per-request skew.

3) batch_position: batch_position is the ordinal position in the batch at dequeue time; it is deterministic for the runtime's scheduling policy during that test run but not guaranteed across restarts. For interleaving harnesses capture both batch_id and batch_position; join on request_id to attribute accurately.

Recommended pasteable RFP language (short):

-- Instrumentation fields we expect the vendor to provide (one row per request):
- request_id (string UUID)
- t_client_send (ISO8601)
- t_ingress_recv (ISO8601)
- t_runtime_start (ISO8601)
- t_model_start (ISO8601)
- t_model_end (ISO8601)
- t_runtime_send (ISO8601)
- t_client_recv (ISO8601)
- token_count (int)
- synthetic_probe (boolean)
- synthetic_profile (string)
- cache_hit_count (int)
- batch_id (string)
- batch_position (int)
- model_variant (string)

-- Sampling & aggregation guidance:
- Export per-request traces to a streaming store; compute compute-only and network-only latencies separately.
- Use reservoir sampling for p99.9 when QPS > 20k to bound storage.
- Provide a rolling 1h windowed quantile for operational tail analysis and a full-test-run quantile for gating acceptance criteria.

If helpful we can run a 30-minute cross-harness run with your team and annotate the CSV together. Monica / Ben — can you own scheduling and share the QuickStart CLI command and minimal config? Kimberly — flag any security/ingress constraints for Stratus so we send the proper allowlist.

Thanks — happy to jump on a short 30m call if the SREs want to walk the CSV.

Best,
Aditya

--
Aditya Rao
Head of Solutions — Redwood Inference
aditya@redwood.com
Phone: +1 (415) 555-0192

-- Instrumentation fields we expect the vendor to provide (one row per request):
- request_id (string UUID)
- t_client_send (ISO8601)
- t_ingress_recv (ISO8601)
- t_runtime_start (ISO8601)
- t_model_start (ISO8601)
- t_model_end (ISO8601)
- t_runtime_send (ISO8601)
- t_client_recv (ISO8601)
- token_count (int)
- synthetic_probe (boolean)
- synthetic_profile (string)
- cache_hit_count (int)
- batch_id (string)
- batch_position (int)
- model_variant (string)

-- Sampling & aggregation guidance:
- Export per-request traces to a streaming store; compute compute-only and network-only latencies separately.
- Use reservoir sampling for p99.9 when QPS > 20k to bound storage.
- Provide a rolling 1h windowed quantile for operational tail analysis and a full-test-run quantile for gating acceptance criteria.

If helpful we can run a 30-minute cross-harness run with your team and annotate the CSV together. Monica / Ben — can you own scheduling and share the QuickStart CLI command and minimal config? Kimberly — flag any security/ingress constraints for Stratus so we send the proper allowlist.

Thanks — happy to jump on a short 30m call if the SREs want to walk the CSV.

Best,
Aditya

--
Aditya Rao
Head of Solutions — Redwood Inference
aditya@redwood.com
Phone: +1 (415) 555-0192

messages:
From: Sergio Alvarez <sergio.alvarez@redwood.com>
To: Luke Harper <luke.harper@acme.ai>
Cc: Marissa Cole <marissa.cole@redwood.com>, Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwood.com>
Date: Sun, 05 Sep 2027 15:12:00 +0000
Subject: Post-call: multi-hyperscaler delta & unit-econ action plan

Luke / Lauren — thanks again for the call today. Quick recap and proposed next steps so we stay aligned: 

Summary of notes from today
- Primary workload: conversational + short embeddings (avg request ~64 tokens, 70/30 split conv/embed).
- SLOs you called out: p95 latency <= 250ms (interactive), sustained 40–60 QPS at peak.
- Security: you need VPC/private deployment option for the pilot; procurement will ask for SOC2 artifacts.

Competitive / unit-econ snapshot (ballpark, for discussion)
- Redwood (quantized + prefix cache): ~$19 per 1M tokens (assumes 40% cache hit, batching size ~8).
- Hyperscaler A: ~$38 per 1M tokens (no prefix cache for your traffic pattern).
- Hyperscaler B: ~$27 per 1M tokens (better batching but higher latency tail in our tests).

Key technical differentiators we think matter for Acme
- Prefix/KV caching reduces token work for repeated prompts (useful for your 70% repeated intents).
- Continuous batching keeps latency low while improving GPU utilization at your concurrency profile.
- Model variant pinning + automatic fallbacks to cheaper quantized variants for cost predictability.

Concrete next tests we propose
1) 1M token run on your sample dataset (mixed conv+embed) to gather real cache-hit and per-token costs.
2) 30-min stress run at 60 QPS to measure p95 latency and tail behavior with client-like concurrency.
3) Compare identical model bits on Redwood vs Hyperscaler B with same batching and quantization settings; capture token counts and latency histograms.

Attachments: redwood-multi-hyperscaler-delta.pdf (delta table + quick notes), redwood-unit-econ-assumptions.xlsx (assumptions used for the $/1M numbers).

Requests / asks from Acme
- Share a small sample (20–50 representative requests) and an S3 link or upload to the Drive link below so we can seed the cache.
- Confirm whether procurement requires a pre-signed NDA before we run anything on a private endpoint.

I can run the 1M-token job on our side once we have the sample and a short test spec from you (tokenization rules, average prompt length, and target concurrency). If you'd rather, we can give you a short-lived test endpoint and you can run the harness — either works.

Next steps:
- Luke: confirm sample share and security notes.
- Acme procurement: confirm NDA / SSO requirements.
- Redwood: Marissa + Ben to size the lab run and produce a short appendix with assumptions.

Thanks — Sergio

--
Sergio Alvarez
Enterprise Sales, Redwood Inference
sergio.alvarez@redwood.com | +1 (650) 555-0159
From: Luke Harper <luke.harper@acme.ai>
To: Sergio Alvarez <sergio.alvarez@redwood.com>
Cc: Lauren Kim <lauren.kim@acme.ai>
Date: Sun, 05 Sep 2027 19:03:00 +0000
Subject: Re: Post-call: multi-hyperscaler delta & unit-econ action plan

Thanks, Sergio — this is very helpful and captures the call.

We can share a 40-request sample set (anonymized) and a larger S3 bucket with 10k requests for the 1M-token run. I'll check with procurement on the NDA; they usually ask for a one-page pilot MSA. Security-wise, VPC access is preferred but for the initial econ run we can use a short-lived test endpoint if you provide a secure token.

A couple clarifying questions before we hand off to procurement: 
- Can you confirm which model variant you used to produce the $19/1M number? (we want to compare apples-to-apples)
- For the stress run: is there any warmup pattern you recommend to get realistic cache behavior?

I'll get the sample uploaded by EOD tomorrow if procurement doesn't block it.

— Luke
Luke Harper | Head of ML Systems, Acme AI
luke.harper@acme.ai | +1 (415) 555-2222
From: Marissa Cole <marissa.cole@redwood.com>
To: Sergio Alvarez <sergio.alvarez@redwood.com>, Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwood.com>
Date: Mon, 06 Sep 2027 13:11:00 +0000
Subject: Re: Post-call: multi-hyperscaler delta & unit-econ action plan

Sergio — noted. Ben, can you own the lab runs and the unit-econ workbook? I sketched an assumptions tab in redwood-unit-econ-assumptions.xlsx (attached) — please update with real GPU pricing and batching profiles.

Assumptions we should model (placeholders in sheet):
- Model: redwood-instruct-7b-int8 (quantized path) — call this "7b-q" in the sheet.
- Avg request tokens: 64 (prompt + response).
- Cache hit scenarios: 20%, 40%, 60%.
- Batch sizes to simulate: 1, 4, 8, 16.
- Concurrency curves: 10, 30, 60 QPS.

Lab schedule suggestion:
- Wed 09/08 09:00–11:00 PT: run 1M token job + 30-min stress at 60 QPS.
- Wed 09/08 14:00–15:00 PT: quick sync to review outputs and decide hyperscaler comparison knobs.

Ben — if you can populate the sheet with cost per 1M tokens under the different cache/batch combos by EOD Tue, we can share the numbers with Luke ahead of procurement.

Thanks — Marissa

> On Sun, Sep 5, 2027 at 15:12, Sergio Alvarez <sergio.alvarez@redwood.com> wrote:
> > Luke / Lauren — thanks again for the call today. Quick recap and proposed next steps so we stay aligned: 
> > ...
From: Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwood.com>
To: Marissa Cole <marissa.cole@redwood.com>, Sergio Alvarez <sergio.alvarez@redwood.com>
Date: Tue, 07 Sep 2027 09:02:00 +0000
Subject: Re: Post-call: multi-hyperscaler delta & unit-econ action plan

Marissa — taking this on. Quick preliminary numbers based on the sheet and our current internal pricing: 
- Baseline assumptions: 7b-q, avg 64 tokens, 40% cache hit, batch size 8, GPU amortized cost = $1.20 / hour-equivalent per thousand tokens (internal blended).
- Resulting estimated cost: ~$21 per 1M tokens (internal run, includes infra, networking, and basic cache savings).
- Sensitivity: with 60% cache hit -> ~$15 / 1M tokens; with 20% cache hit -> ~$28 / 1M tokens.

Bench plan checklist I'm using:
- Confirm tokenization matches Acme's pipeline (they mentioned custom tokenizer tweaks).
- Seed cache with 10k representative requests before the 1M run (we get better steady-state numbers).
- Capture latency histograms (p50/p95/p99) and per-call token counts.
- Export raw logs (tokenized requests + response token counts) to the Drive link for audit.

I can populate the workbook and upload updated tabbed runs by EOD Tue. Sergio — do you want me to spin the short-lived test endpoint for Acme (they can hit it directly) or run the lab entirely on our side? I recommend we run on our side first to avoid credentials back-and-forth.

Ben Carter
Solutions Engineer, Redwood Inference
ben.carter@redwood.com | +1 (650) 555-0175

source.subject: Transfer playbook: DPA redline sprint and SCCs mapping
messages:
From: Sarah Wells <sarah.wells@brightlane.com>
To: Alex Martinez <alex.martinez@redwood.ai>
Cc: Brightlane Contracts <contracts@brightlane.com>
Date: 2027-09-06T08:15:00-07:00
Subject: DPA redline and SCCs: transfer addendum questions

Hi Alex —

Thanks for sending over the draft MSA and order form. Our legal team reviewed the DPA and attached a marked-up redline (attached). A few high-priority asks from our side: 

- Controller/Processor role: the redline still treats Brightlane as Controller and Redwood as Processor, but our GC flagged one Order Form clause that could make us joint controllers for certain enrichment flows. Can you confirm current data flow ownership for enrichment and model telemetry?
- SCCs annex: we need the EU SCC Module 2 replacement annex; your draft references an internal exhibit. Please confirm you can sign SCCs Section B (processor addendum) and provide the annex version you intend to execute.
- Transfer Impact Addendum (TIA): given the planned cross-border routing, our privacy lead wants a TIA with a subprocessor inventory and hosting map (regions + fallback). If you can provide a TIA draft we can speed review.
- KMS/key management: Brightlane prefers customer-managed keys for egress-sensitive data. Do you support bring-your-own-key (BYOK) for Dedicated/Private? If not, what mitigations (encryption, HSM) can we commit to?
- Evidence & timeline: can you provide SOC2 scope confirmation (is model training part of scope?) and a 2-week turnaround on a consolidated DPA redline response?

Proposed next step: 30-minute sync with Alex + Redwood legal + Brightlane GC tomorrow or Friday to walk the redlines. We can share an internal tracker if that helps.

Attachment: Brightlane_DPA_redline_v1.docx (docx, 92 KB)

Thanks,
Sarah Wells
Sr. Counsel, Brightlane Health

--
Brightlane Contracts: contracts@brightlane.com
From: Alex Martinez <alex.martinez@redwood.ai>
To: Laura Bennett <laura.bennett@redwood.ai>, Karthik Iyer <karthik.iyer@redwood.ai>, Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwood.ai>, Kimberly Park <kimberly.park@redwood.ai>
Date: 2027-09-06T09:05:00-07:00
Subject: Re: DPA redline and SCCs: transfer addendum questions

Team — looping you in. See Brightlane's marked redline attached to their note below. Key asks summarized: 

1) Confirm controller/processor boundary for enrichment & telemetry flows (Brightlane thinks potential joint controller language present).
2) Execute Processor SCCs annex compatible with EU Module 2 (they want explicit SCC Module reference).
3) Provide a TIA template plus subprocessor inventory and hosting fallback map.
4) Clarify KMS/BYOK support and fallback mitigations for Dedicated/Private.
5) SOC 2 scope confirmation and a 2-week turnaround on consolidated DPA response.

Karthik — can you take lead on legal redline comments and propose language for SCCs and TIA? If we need any non-standard commitments (BYOK, additional audits), flag commercial impact.
Laura — FYI for exec visibility in case Brightlane pushes for product/hosting concessions.
Ben — please coordinate the call and be prepared to confirm the commercial runway for any add-on (KMS, Private) they request.
Kimberly — could you run an infra check on whether BYOK is available for Dedicated and what the engineering ETA looks like for a scoped BYOK path or HSM alternative?

I propose we produce a consolidated response and draft TIA by EOD Friday (2-week cadence is the ask; we should aim for quicker if possible). Please reply with owners/estimates.

On Mon, Sep 6, 2027 at 8:15 AM Sarah Wells <sarah.wells@brightlane.com> wrote:
> Hi Alex —
> 
> Thanks for sending over the draft MSA and order form. Our legal team reviewed the DPA and attached a marked-up redline (attached). A few high-priority asks from our side: ...
>
> Attachment: Brightlane_DPA_redline_v1.docx (docx, 92 KB)

Best,
Alex Martinez
Senior Solutions Engineer | Redwood Inference
From: Karthik Iyer <karthik.iyer@redwood.ai>
To: Alex Martinez <alex.martinez@redwood.ai>, Laura Bennett <laura.bennett@redwood.ai>, Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwood.ai>, Kimberly Park <kimberly.park@redwood.ai>
Date: 2027-09-06T11:30:00-07:00
Subject: RE: DPA redline and SCCs: transfer addendum questions

Alex / team —

I reviewed Brightlane's redline and the asks. High-level position and proposed edits below (I'll prepare a tracked redline and a short memo by EOD):

1) Controller / Processor: our standard model is Brightlane = Controller; Redwood = Processor for inference and embeddings. For enrichment/telemetry (model feature engineering, telemetry logs containing customer PII), we need to narrow the Order Form language to specify that any "data enrichment" work will be performed only on data to which the customer has granted explicit processing rights and that Redwood will process that data as a Processor acting on documented instructions. I propose: add clause in Order Form SOW: "Where Customer supplies data for enrichment, Customer represents it has lawfully collected and may transfer such data and instructs Redwood to act as Processor for the enrichment service; Redwood will not determine the purposes and means of processing."

2) SCCs annex: we can sign the Processor Module (Module 2 equivalent). Recommend attaching the European Commission's 2021 SCCs processor addendum as Exhibit A and include a short table mapping our subprocessors and hosting locations. We should avoid bespoke language that reintroduces controller obligations. I'll prepare the Exhibit with mapped fields.

3) TIA (Transfer Impact Assessment): we should produce a short TIA covering: hosting regions, fallback routing, subprocessors list, encryption in transit & at rest, access controls, and documented audit frequency. I'll draft a 1-page TIA template and populate with our current infra map.

4) KMS / BYOK: BYOK for Dedicated is feasible for Private customer deployments but not for hosted shared tier today. For Dedicated pools we support customer-managed keys via an HSM-backed KMS; we'll need an SC/eng estimate for onboarding . For the Hosted plan we can offer encryption-at-rest + HSM-backed keys managed by Redwood. Kimberly, please provide a clear bullet with: (a) BYOK support yes/no for Dedicated/Private, (b) engineering ETA and any extra run-rate OPEX.

6) Proposed timeline: draft TIA + tracked DPA redline by Friday EOD; 30-minute call to walk open points — suggested slots: Fri 10:00-10:30 AM BST or Mon 9:00-9:30 AM BST. Ben — propose final slot to Brightlane.

From: Karthik Iyer <karthik.iyer@redwood.ai>
To: Alex Martinez <alex.martinez@redwood.ai>, Laura Bennett <laura.bennett@redwood.ai>, Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwood.ai>, Kimberly Park <kimberly.park@redwood.ai>
Date: 2027-09-06T11:30:00-07:00
Subject: RE: DPA redline and SCCs: transfer addendum questions

Alex / team —

I reviewed Brightlane's redline and the asks. High-level position and proposed edits below (I'll prepare a tracked redline and a short memo by EOD):

1) Controller / Processor: our standard model is Brightlane = Controller; Redwood = Processor for inference and embeddings. For enrichment/telemetry (model feature engineering, telemetry logs containing customer PII), we need to narrow the Order Form language to specify that any "data enrichment" work will be performed only on data to which the customer has granted explicit processing rights and that Redwood will process that data as a Processor acting on documented instructions. I propose: add clause in Order Form SOW: "Where Customer supplies data for enrichment, Customer represents it has lawfully collected and may transfer such data and instructs Redwood to act as Processor for the enrichment service; Redwood will not determine the purposes and means of processing."

2) SCCs annex: we can sign the Processor Module (Module 2 equivalent). Recommend attaching the European Commission's 2021 SCCs processor addendum as Exhibit A and include a short table mapping our subprocessors and hosting locations. We should avoid bespoke language that reintroduces controller obligations. I'll prepare the Exhibit with mapped fields.

3) TIA (Transfer Impact Assessment): we should produce a short TIA covering: hosting regions, fallback routing, subprocessors list, encryption in transit & at rest, access controls, and documented audit frequency. I'll draft a 1-page TIA template and populate with our current infra map.

4) KMS / BYOK: BYOK for Dedicated is feasible for Private customer deployments but not for hosted shared tier today. For Dedicated pools we support customer-managed keys via an HSM-backed KMS; we'll need an SC/eng estimate for onboarding . For the Hosted plan we can offer encryption-at-rest + HSM-backed keys managed by Redwood. Kimberly, please provide a clear bullet with: (a) BYOK support yes/no for Dedicated/Private, (b) engineering ETA and any extra run-rate OPEX.

5) SOC 2 scope: confirm that our SOC 2 type II coverage includes production inference endpoints and data storage (not model training sandbox). If model training is in scope, we need to clarify which datasets are in scope. Ops, please confirm scope and provide an SOC2 summary we can share.

6) Proposed timeline: draft TIA + tracked DPA redline by Friday EOD; 30-minute call to walk open points — suggested slots: Fri 10:00-10:30 AM BST or Mon 9:00-9:30 AM BST. Ben — propose final slot to Brightlane.

Attachment: KMS-evidence-notes.txt (txt, 6 KB)

Regards,
Karthik Iyer
Senior Counsel, Redwood Inference

source.subject: Consumption health review and scaled-term recommendation — Nebula Labs
messages:
From: Tara Singh <tara@nebulalabs.com>
To: Alex Martinez <alex.martinez@redwood.com>
Cc: Rajiv Menon <rajiv.menon@nebulalabs.com>
Date: Mon, 16 Aug 2027 08:14:00 -0700
Subject: Q3 - Request: usage health + renewal conversation

Hi Alex,

Quick note from Nebula: we've seen a sustained spike in generation volume the last 6 weeks tied to a new feature we launched. We're approaching our committed run-rate and want to make sure we aren't going to hit throttles mid-quarter. High-level context:~42M tokens in trailing 30 days (aggregate), 60% of requests include long contexts (~1.2k tokens), and latency-sensitive workflows are our priority.

Can you do a short health/usage review and recommend options for scaling (commit increase vs dedicated pools)? We'd also like to understand the unit-economics and what a 12-month uplift would look like (savings vs current on-demand).

Attached: nebula-q3-usage.csv (request-level counts).

Would love a 30-min sync this week if possible. Our ASCT is 10am PT on Wed or Thu.

Thanks,
Tara
Tara Singh
Head of Product, Nebula Labs
From: Alex Martinez <alex.martinez@redwood.com>
To: Tara Singh <tara@nebulalabs.com>
Cc: Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwood.com>, Monica Patel <monica.patel@redwood.com>
Date: Mon, 16 Aug 2027 10:02:00 -0700
Subject: Re: Q3 - Request: usage health + renewal conversation

Hi Tara — thanks for the data, that helps a lot. I pulled together an initial health snapshot and a few quick observations; attaching nebula-q3-health-report.csv for your review.

High-level summary (trailing 30 days):
- Total generated tokens: ~42,500,000
- Median tokens / request: ~350; 95th percentile ~1,150 (matches your long-context note)
- P95 latency (hosted): 180ms for short contexts, up to 480ms for >1k contexts
- Error/retry rate: 0.2% (no spiky degradation seen)
- Caching opportunities: ~18% of requests share stable prefixes where KV/prefix caching could reduce tokens by ~12%

Economics & options:
1) Increase committed monthly tokens (12-month term): projected 30% unit-cost reduction vs on-demand once committed tier crossed; breaks even in ~6 weeks given your current run-rate.
2) Move hot-paths to a Dedicated pool (reserved GPU capacity): better P95 latency (~35–45% improvement for long contexts) and predictable tail latency — adds fixed monthly fee but reduces per-token variable cost after overhead.
3) Hybrid: commit for baseline + Dedicated for latency-sensitive routes (auto-fallback to hosted).

Next steps I recommend:
- Internal alignment: Ben (sales) and Monica (CS) will draft a proposal with committed tiers and a Dedicated sizing recommendation.
- Customer sync: Wed 10am PT works for a quick alignment call; will send invite.

Attachments: nebula-q3-health-report.csv (request-level breakdown, latency histograms, prefix-opportunity table).

Best,
Alex Martinez
Customer Success & Sales Engineer
Redwood Inference

On Mon, Aug 16, 2027 at 08:14 AM Tara Singh <tara@nebulalabs.com> wrote:
> Hi Alex,
> 
> Quick note from Nebula: we've seen a sustained spike...
From: Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwood.com>
To: Alex Martinez <alex.martinez@redwood.com>, Monica Patel <monica.patel@redwood.com>
Cc: Marissa Cole <marissa.cole@redwood.com>
Date: Tue, 17 Aug 2027 09:20:00 -0700
Subject: Nebula proposal - draft math and asks

Alex / Monica — looping Marissa for pricing approval. Draft thinking after reviewing Alex's health report: 

Assumptions (current trailing 30d): 42.5M tokens => run-rate ~1.275B tokens / month annualized (note: we price monthly commits, not annualized, but for elasticity calc this helps).
Proposed committed tiers: baseline 50M tokens / month with 12-month term (covers current + buffer), scaled up to 75M tokens / month for predictable growth windows.

Pricing model (illustrative):
- Current on-demand blended cost: ~$8.00 / 1M generated tokens (blended across models and contexts).
- 12-mo commit (50M/mo): $5.60 / 1M (30% discount) => monthly savings ~ $1080 on typical month, larger on high-use months due to tiering.
- Dedicated pool option: $Xk/mo fixed + $3.50 / 1M for in-pool variable use (after amortizing fixed infra). For latency-sensitive traffic we estimate 28% cost reduction vs hosted on-demand when amortized at your volumes.

Special concessions to accelerate decision: one-time migration credit $10,000, and a 90-day rate guarantee for overages during initial ramp.

I'll attach a clean proposal PDF (proposal-draft.pdf) after Marissa signs off. Please confirm if the baseline 50M token/month tier fits your forecast or if we should size to 65M.

-ben
Ben Carter
Account Executive, Redwood Inference

> On Mon, Aug 16, 2027 at 10:02 AM Alex Martinez <alex.martinez@redwood.com> wrote:
> > Hi Tara — thanks for the data...
From: Alex Martinez <alex.martinez@redwood.com>
To: Tara Singh <tara@nebulalabs.com>, Rajiv Menon <rajiv.menon@nebulalabs.com>
Cc: Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwood.com>, Monica Patel <monica.patel@redwood.com>, Marissa Cole <marissa.cole@redwood.com>
Date: Wed, 18 Aug 2027 16:02:00 -0700
Subject: Q3 Health recap + recommended term & next steps

Tara / Rajiv — thanks again for the quick sync. Below is the consolidated recap and our recommended next steps to keep Nebula stable through the product ramp while reducing unit costs.

Executive summary:
- Trailing 30d tokens: ~42.5M (median request size ~350 tokens; 95th ~1.15k). Long-context requests are the main driver of latency and variable cost.
- Performance: hosted P95 latency for long contexts ~480ms; Dedicated pool estimated P95 ~260–300ms for same workloads.
- Cost: current blended on-demand ~ $8 / 1M generated tokens. 12-mo 50M/mo commit reduces to ~$5.60 / 1M (estimated). Hybrid with Dedicated for hot routes can further reduce effective cost to ~$4.00–$4.50 / 1M after amortizing infra for your scale.

Proposal (what we're asking):
1) 12-month committed baseline: 50M tokens / month (covers current run-rate + buffer)
2) 3-month trial of Dedicated pool for latency-sensitive API routes (sized for 10–15% of traffic)
3) One-time migration credit: $10,000; 90-day overage rate guarantee

Projected impact (simple ROI):
- With the commit, Nebula would save ~30% on token spend vs current on-demand at current volumes. If you accept the hybrid Dedicated plan for hot traffic, total monthly spend could decline ~35–40% while improving P95 latency by ~35%.

Attached: nebula-q3-health-report.csv, proposal-draft.pdf.
DocuSign link (term addendum): https://docusign.com/link/nebula-term-addendum-42

Next steps:
- If this looks good, I can route the addendum to Rajiv for signature and get engineering onboarded with Dedicated pool configuration.
- Proposed time to finalize: by end of Aug (we can turn up Dedicated trial in ~2 weeks after signature).

Looking forward — happy to jump on a follow-up to walk through the cost math.

Best,
Alex Martinez
Customer Success & Sales Engineer
Redwood Inference

> On Tue, Aug 17, 2027 at 09:20 AM Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwood.com> wrote:
> > Alex / Monica — looping Marissa for pricing approval...

From: Tara Singh <tara@nebulalabs.com>
To: Alex Martinez <alex.martinez@redwood.com>
Cc: Rajiv Menon <rajiv.menon@nebulalabs.com>
Date: Mon, 16 Aug 2027 08:14:00 -0700
Subject: Q3 - Request: usage health + renewal conversation

Hi Alex,

Quick note from Nebula: we've seen a sustained spike in generation volume the last 6 weeks tied to a new feature we launched. We're approaching our committed run-rate and want to make sure we aren't going to hit throttles mid-quarter. High-level context:~42M tokens in trailing 30 days (aggregate), 60% of requests include long contexts (~1.2k tokens), and latency-sensitive workflows are our priority.

Can you do a short health/usage review and recommend options for scaling (commit increase vs dedicated pools)? We'd also like to understand the unit-economics and what a 12-month uplift would look like (savings vs current on-demand).

Attached: nebula-q3-usage.csv (request-level counts).

Would love a 30-min sync this week if possible. Our ASCT is 10am PT on Wed or Thu.

Thanks,
Tara
Tara Singh
Head of Product, Nebula Labs


From: Alex Martinez <alex.martinez@redwood.com>
To: Tara Singh <tara@nebulalabs.com>
Cc: Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwood.com>, Monica Patel <monica.patel@redwood.com>
Date: Mon, 16 Aug 2027 10:02:00 -0700
Subject: Re: Q3 - Request: usage health + renewal conversation

Hi Tara — thanks for the data, that helps a lot. I pulled together an initial health snapshot and a few quick observations; attaching nebula-q3-health-report.csv for your review.

High-level summary (trailing 30 days):
- Total generated tokens: ~42,500,000
- Median tokens / request: ~350; 95th percentile ~1,150 (matches your long-context note)
- P95 latency (hosted): 180ms for short contexts, up to 480ms for >1k contexts
- Error/retry rate: 0.2% (no spiky degradation seen)
- Caching opportunities: ~18% of requests share stable prefixes where KV/prefix caching could reduce tokens by ~12%

Economics & options:
1) Increase committed monthly tokens (12-month term): projected 30% unit-cost reduction vs on-demand once committed tier crossed; breaks even in ~6 weeks given your current run-rate.
2) Move hot-paths to a Dedicated pool (reserved GPU capacity): better P95 latency (~35–45% improvement for long contexts) and predictable tail latency — adds fixed monthly fee but reduces per-token variable cost after overhead.
3) Hybrid: commit for baseline + Dedicated for latency-sensitive routes (auto-fallback to hosted).

Next steps I recommend:
- Internal alignment: Ben (sales) and Monica (CS) will draft a proposal with committed tiers and a Dedicated sizing recommendation.
- Customer sync: Wed 10am PT works for a quick alignment call; will send invite.

Attachments: nebula-q3-health-report.csv (request-level breakdown, latency histograms, prefix-opportunity table).

Best,
Alex Martinez
Customer Success & Sales Engineer
Redwood Inference

messages:
From: Alex Martinez <alex.martinez@redwood.ai>
To: Samir Shah <samir@acmehealth.com>
Cc: Marissa Cole <marissa.cole@redwood.ai>
Date: Tue, 03 Aug 2027 15:12:00 -0700
Subject: Post-demo: Acme Health - credentials & integration decision options

Hi Samir —

Thanks again for the time today. Quick write-up of what we covered and two recommended paths for credentials so your security and procurement teams can evaluate quickly.

Recap (short):
- Demo walk-through: Redwood hosted API + optional Dedicated pool for production throughput.
- Key integration points for Acme: SDK bootstrap in backend services, browser-safe flows for clinicians, embeddings for semantic search, and a re-ranker for prioritization.

Credentials options we recommend (tradeoffs):
1) Service-account JWT (recommended for backend services)
   - Description: Use JWT-signed service account credentials with short-lived bearer tokens issued by Redwood’s token service.
   - Pros: Strong auditability, supports automated rotation via KMS, easy to integrate with server-side SDKs, works well for embedding + reranker calls.
   - Cons: Requires a one-time provisioning step and KMS/HSM integration for private key storage if you require hardware-backed keys.

2) API keys + short-lived session tokens for browser use (where needed)
   - Description: Issued from a backend exchange endpoint. Do not embed static API keys in client apps.
   - Pros: Simpler initial dev experience, lower friction for early POCs.
   - Cons: Higher operational risk if used as long-lived keys; procurement will likely push for rotation and scoped keys.

Attachments: sample SDK bootstrapping file (redwood-sdk-sample-config.yaml) and an initial SOW draft attached for procurement review.

Next steps (proposed):
- You confirm which credential approach you prefer (JWT service account vs API-key-first) — target by EOD Thu 2027-08-05.
- If JWT: schedule a 30-min security call to cover KMS/HSM and audit log access (we can include our security engineer).
- If API keys: we will provide a short-lived token exchange example and extra guardrails for key rotation cadence.

Would love to loop procurement/contact person when you’re ready. I cc’d Marissa for pricing implications.

Thanks,
Alex Martinez
Solutions Engineer | Redwood Inference
redwood.ai

Attachments: redwood-sdk-sample-config.yaml (text/yaml), acme-sow-draft.pdf (application/pdf)
From: Samir Shah <samir@acmehealth.com>
To: Alex Martinez <alex.martinez@redwood.ai>
Cc: Nora Li <nora.li@procureops.com>
Date: Wed, 04 Aug 2027 09:04:00 -0700
Subject: Re: Post-demo: Acme Health - credentials & integration decision options

Alex —

Great summary, thanks. A couple quick clarifying Qs from our security/procurement side: 

- Rotation cadence: For the JWT approach, what is the recommended key rotation interval and do you support automated rotation via a cloud KMS (we use GCP KMS)?
- HSM requirement: Procurement indicated they prefer HSM-backed keys for production secrets. Can Redwood's token service accept public keys stored in an external KMS/HSM or do we need to host the signing service?
- Data access model: When we call embeddings and reranker, where does the model execution happen and what logs are retained? We need an audit trail for queries and a retention policy.

Also: we prefer to avoid embedding static API keys in any client. We will likely want the JWT approach for server-to-server flows and a browser short-lived-exchange for clinician UIs.

I can put Nora (procurement) on a thread for SOW and compliance docs. Please share any SOC2 / audit evidence links.

Thanks,
Samir Shah
Head of Engineering, Acme Health
samir@acmehealth.com

---
Acme is HIPAA-conscious; all PII/PHI must be handled per our security checklist.
From: Alex Martinez <alex.martinez@redwood.ai>
To: Samir Shah <samir@acmehealth.com>
Cc: Marissa Cole <marissa.cole@redwood.ai>, Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwood.ai>
Date: Thu, 05 Aug 2027 10:28:00 -0700
Subject: Re: Post-demo: Acme Health - credentials & integration decision options

Samir —

Answers inline below. Short version: JWT service account + KMS-backed private keys is the recommended path for production; we support it and can map to your GCP KMS.

- Rotation cadence: Recommended 30–90 days for signing keys; we support automated rotation hooks and can integrate with GCP KMS for key lifecycle. We also support issuing short-lived tokens (15m to 1h) to callers so the signing key rotation is decoupled from token lifetime.
- HSM: Redwood supports external KMS/HSM integrations. For GCP KMS we can store the signing key material in KMS and configure Redwood to call KMS-based signing. If procurement requires a fully customer-managed signing service (air-gapped or on-prem), our Private deployment path supports that.
- Logs & retention: For hosted API, we retain audit logs (request ids, timestamps, model variant, token identity) for 90 days by default; we can extend retention for enterprise contracts. Logs do not contain raw PHI by default — customers can enable encrypted request/response logging if needed and agree on an encrypted storage lifecycle.

Attachments: redwood-rotation-playbook.pdf (explains rotation, token lifetimes, and example KMS config).

Implementation notes / SDK bits:
- Backend: use our server SDK (see sample config attached) to exchange a service-account JWT for a short-lived bearer token used for all Redwood API calls.
- Browser flows: implement a backend token-exchange endpoint that mints ephemeral scoped tokens for clinicians (no long-lived keys in client).
- Embeddings/rerank: call with the service token; we recommend scoping embedding model calls to a dedicated model alias and enabling prefix caching for performance.

If this matches your expected security posture, we can:
1) Prepare a revised SOW reflecting KMS/HSM and extended log retention (I attached the SOW draft).
2) Schedule a 30-min security deep-dive with our security engineer (propose Fri 2027-08-06 10:00 PDT).

Next step for you: confirm preference for JWT-based service accounts and whether to include GCP KMS integration in the SOW.

—Alex
From: Marissa Cole <marissa.cole@redwood.ai>
To: Samir Shah <samir@acmehealth.com>, Alex Martinez <alex.martinez@redwood.ai>
Cc: Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwood.ai>
Date: Fri, 06 Aug 2027 08:15:00 -0700
Subject: Re: Post-demo: Acme Health - credentials & integration decision options

Samir —

Looping in Ben from Solutions/Infra to flag cost and dedicated capacity implications. Quick notes on commercial side: 
- If you choose JWT + KMS-backed keys and require 90-day log retention + dedicated GPU pool, we will include a Dedicated proposal in the SOW (monthly committed throughput).
- If you want hosted with standard retention, we can do a lower-touch plan and shorten commercial lead time.

Ben, can you share a ballpark for dedicated pool pricing and any lead times for a GCP-region deployment? Samir, happy to work with Nora on procurement and finalize the SOW.

Thanks,
Marissa Cole
Head of Sales | Redwood Inference

From: Samir Shah <samir@acmehealth.com>
To: Marissa Cole <marissa.cole@redwood.ai>, Alex Martinez <alex.martinez@redwood.ai>
Cc: Nora Li <nora.li@procureops.com>
Date: Fri, 06 Aug 2027 12:30:00 -0700
Subject: Re: Post-demo: Acme Health - credentials & integration decision options

Marissa / Alex —

Thanks all. We prefer to proceed with the JWT service-account approach integrated with our GCP KMS for production (HSM-backed keys). Please include:
- KMS integration and signing details in the SOW
- 90-day audit log retention and optional export to our SIEM
- Scoped tokens for browser token exchange pattern (no static client keys)

Procurement contact: Nora Li (nora.li@procureops.com). Please send the updated SOW and I will shepherd it through approval. We are ready to sign once the KMS and retention items are explicit.

DocuSign link for quick turnaround: https://docusign.example.com/ACM-1342-sow

Samir Shah
Head of Engineering, Acme Health

Attachments referenced: none in this reply.

   - Cons: Higher operational risk if used as long-lived keys; procurement will likely push for rotation and scoped keys.

Attachments: sample SDK bootstrapping file (redwood-sdk-sample-config.yaml) and an initial SOW draft attached for procurement review.

Next steps (proposed):
- You confirm which credential approach you prefer (JWT service account vs API-key-first) — target by EOD Thu 2027-08-05.
- If JWT: schedule a 30-min security call to cover KMS/HSM and audit log access (we can include our security engineer).
- If API keys: we will provide a short-lived token exchange example and extra guardrails for key rotation cadence.

Would love to loop procurement/contact person when you’re ready. I cc’d Marissa for pricing implications.

Thanks,
Alex Martinez
Solutions Engineer | Redwood Inference
redwood.ai

Attachments: redwood-sdk-sample-config.yaml (text/yaml), acme-sow-draft.pdf (application/pdf)


From: Samir Shah <samir@acmehealth.com>
To: Alex Martinez <alex.martinez@redwood.ai>
Cc: Nora Li <nora.li@procureops.com>
Date: Wed, 04 Aug 2027 09:04:00 -0700
Subject: Re: Post-demo: Acme Health - credentials & integration decision options

Alex —

Great summary, thanks. A couple quick clarifying Qs from our security/procurement side: 

- Rotation cadence: For the JWT approach, what is the recommended key rotation interval and do you support automated rotation via a cloud KMS (we use GCP KMS)?
- HSM requirement: Procurement indicated they prefer HSM-backed keys for production secrets. Can Redwood's token service accept public keys stored in an external KMS/HSM or do we need to host the signing service?
- Data access model: When we call embeddings and reranker, where does the model execution happen and what logs are retained? We need an audit trail for queries and a retention policy.

Also: we prefer to avoid embedding static API keys in any client. We will likely want the JWT approach for server-to-server flows and a browser short-lived-exchange for clinician UIs.

I can put Nora (procurement) on a thread for SOW and compliance docs. Please share any SOC2 / audit evidence links.

Thanks,
Samir Shah
Head of Engineering, Acme Health
samir@acmehealth.com

---
Acme is HIPAA-conscious; all PII/PHI must be handled per our security checklist.


From: Alex Martinez <alex.martinez@redwood.ai>
To: Samir Shah <samir@acmehealth.com>
Cc: Marissa Cole <marissa.cole@redwood.ai>, Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwood.ai>
Date: Thu, 05 Aug 2027 10:28:00 -0700
Subject: Re: Post-demo: Acme Health - credentials & integration decision options

Samir —

Answers inline below. Short version: JWT service account + KMS-backed private keys is the recommended path for production; we support it and can map to your GCP KMS.

- Rotation cadence: Recommended 30–90 days for signing keys; we support automated rotation hooks and can integrate with GCP KMS for key lifecycle. We also support issuing short-lived tokens (15m to 1h) to callers so the signing key rotation is decoupled from token lifetime.
- HSM: Redwood supports external KMS/HSM integrations. For GCP KMS we can store the signing key material in KMS and configure Redwood to call KMS-based signing. If procurement requires a fully customer-managed signing service (air-gapped or on-prem), our Private deployment path supports that.
- Logs & retention: For hosted API, we retain audit logs (request ids, timestamps, model variant, token identity) for 90 days by default; we can extend retention for enterprise contracts. Logs do not contain raw PHI by default — customers can enable encrypted request/response logging if needed and agree on an encrypted storage lifecycle.

Attachments: redwood-rotation-playbook.pdf (explains rotation, token lifetimes, and example KMS config).

Implementation notes / SDK bits:
- Backend: use our server SDK (see sample config attached) to exchange a service-account JWT for a short-lived bearer token used for all Redwood API calls.
- Browser flows: implement a backend token-exchange endpoint that mints ephemeral scoped tokens for clinicians (no long-lived keys in client).
- Embeddings/rerank: call with the service token; we recommend scoping embedding model calls to a dedicated model alias and enabling prefix caching for performance.

If this matches your expected security posture, we can:
1) Prepare a revised SOW reflecting KMS/HSM and extended log retention (I attached the SOW draft).
2) Schedule a 30-min security deep-dive with our security engineer (propose Fri 2027-08-06 10:00 PDT).

Next step for you: confirm preference for JWT-based service accounts and whether to include GCP KMS integration in the SOW.

—Alex


From: Marissa Cole <marissa.cole@redwood.ai>
To: Samir Shah <samir@acmehealth.com>, Alex Martinez <alex.martinez@redwood.ai>
Cc: Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwood.ai>
Date: Fri, 06 Aug 2027 08:15:00 -0700
Subject: Re: Post-demo: Acme Health - credentials & integration decision options

Samir —

Looping in Ben from Solutions/Infra to flag cost and dedicated capacity implications. Quick notes on commercial side: 
- If you choose JWT + KMS-backed keys and require 90-day log retention + dedicated GPU pool, we will include a Dedicated proposal in the SOW (monthly committed throughput).
- If you want hosted with standard retention, we can do a lower-touch plan and shorten commercial lead time.

Ben, can you share a ballpark for dedicated pool pricing and any lead times for a GCP-region deployment? Samir, happy to work with Nora on procurement and finalize the SOW.

Thanks,
Marissa Cole
Head of Sales | Redwood Inference



From: Samir Shah <samir@acmehealth.com>
To: Marissa Cole <marissa.cole@redwood.ai>, Alex Martinez <alex.martinez@redwood.ai>
Cc: Nora Li <nora.li@procureops.com>
Date: Fri, 06 Aug 2027 12:30:00 -0700
Subject: Re: Post-demo: Acme Health - credentials & integration decision options

Marissa / Alex —

Thanks all. We prefer to proceed with the JWT service-account approach integrated with our GCP KMS for production (HSM-backed keys). Please include:
- KMS integration and signing details in the SOW
- 90-day audit log retention and optional export to our SIEM
- Scoped tokens for browser token exchange pattern (no static client keys)

Procurement contact: Nora Li (nora.li@procureops.com). Please send the updated SOW and I will shepherd it through approval. We are ready to sign once the KMS and retention items are explicit.

DocuSign link for quick turnaround: https://docusign.example.com/ACM-1342-sow

Samir Shah
Head of Engineering, Acme Health

Attachments referenced: none in this reply.

source.subject: Re: MAP — Streaming TTFT acceptance & final benchmark
messages:
From: Elena Ruiz <elena@stellahealth.ai>
To: Aisha Rahman <aisha@redwood.inference.com>, Marissa Cole <marissa@redwood.inference.com>
Cc: Procurement <procurement@stellahealth.ai>
Date: Thu, 29 Jul 2027 09:12:00 -0700
Subject: MAP request — TTFT + streaming acceptance criteria and timeline

Hi Aisha / Marissa,

Thanks again for the demo last week. We're ready to move to a short MAP focused on performance acceptance so we can finish procurement. Top asks from our side: clear TTFT (time-to-first-token) and streaming latency targets at our expected concurrency, an evaluation plan we can run in our staging account, and a mutually-agreed close timeline tied to those checks.

High-level constraints:
- Typical session length: 180 tokens output (median)
- Peak concurrency: 12 concurrent chat sessions across our customer-facing gateway
- 95% of interactions should be interactive (< 300ms perceived streaming latency for first 2 tokens)

Can you propose acceptance numbers (p50/p95/p99 TTFT and streaming latencies), test harness details, and a timeline for delivery? We'd like the MAP to land in the next 10 business days if possible. I've attached our draft MAP request doc (stella_map_request_v1.pdf).

Elena
Elena Ruiz
Head of Product
Stella Health

Attachments: stella_map_request_v1.pdf (pdf)

From: Aisha Rahman <aisha@redwood.inference.com>
To: Elena Ruiz <elena@stellahealth.ai>
Cc: Marissa Cole <marissa@redwood.inference.com>
Date: Fri, 30 Jul 2027 11:08:00 -0700
Subject: Re: MAP request — TTFT + streaming acceptance criteria and timeline

Elena — thanks for the clear asks. Looping Marissa (bench team) and Ben (sales ops) into the thread so we can lock the MAP milestones. Below is our proposed MAP summary (we'll put this into a formal MAP PDF and share to HubSpot):

Proposed MAP milestones (fast-track, 10 business days):
1) Day 0 (today): Agree scope & acceptance metrics — this email
2) Day 1–2: Redwood runs internal baseline with a staging trace of Stella traffic shape; share raw results + config
3) Day 3–5: Customer-run evaluation in Stella staging account using Redwood test harness (we provide container + config)
4) Day 6: Joint review; if acceptance criteria met -> sign-off and procurement handoff
5) Day 7–10: Canary rollout window + 72-hour stability observation before production go-live

Acceptance metrics (initial proposal):
- TTFT (streaming enabled, first token delivered): p50 <= 70ms, p95 <= 210ms, p99 <= 420ms
- Streaming latency (inter-token for first 2 tokens perceived by client): p50 <= 35ms, p95 <= 150ms, p99 <= 300ms
- End-to-end completion latency for 180-token median: p50 <= 650ms, p95 <= 1100ms, p99 <= 1600ms
- Concurrency target: steady 12 concurrent sessions, 95% requests should meet p95 targets above

Test harness & config we will provide:
- Containerized harness (k6 + custom streaming client) configured to Stella token distribution
- Pre-built model variant (quantized where appropriate) and kernel-config for matching latency profile
- KV-cache prefixing to mirror typical session warm-starts
- Perf artifact: test-harness-config.json (we will attach)

Deliverables we'll share: baseline CSV of latencies, run scripts, and MAP PDF. Marissa — can you run an internal baseline with the harness and share the CSV? Ben — I'll add the MAP doc to HubSpot and flag procurement.

Thanks,
Aisha Rahman
Director, Solutions
Redwood Inference

Attachments: test-harness-config.json (json)

From: Marissa Cole <marissa@redwood.inference.com>
To: Aisha Rahman <aisha@redwood.inference.com>, Ben Carter <ben@redwood.inference.com>
Cc: Elena Ruiz <elena@stellahealth.ai>, Stella Health SRE <sre@stellahealth.ai>
Date: Mon, 02 Aug 2027 10:22:00 -0700
Subject: Re: MAP request — baseline run and preliminary results

Aisha / team — I ran a dry-run on our staging cluster using the harness configured to Stella's token histogram. Notes and top-line numbers below (attached full CSV). Test run details:
- Model variant: redwood-open-13b-quant-v2 (streaming enabled)
- Hardware: 2xA100 (40GB) equivalent pool, continuous batching disabled for first-token measurement, KV cache warm for 60% of sessions
- Concurrency shape: steady 12 sessions, random jitter (±10%)

Preliminary TTFT & streaming numbers (single-run):
- TTFT p50: 68ms, p95: 198ms, p99: 410ms
- Streaming inter-token (first-2) p50: 32ms, p95: 142ms, p99: 290ms
- End-to-end (180 tokens) p50: 610ms, p95: 1060ms, p99: 1520ms

Observations / risks:
- P99 is sensitive to simultaneous KV-warm misses — recommend we run a Stella-shaped warmup (prefix reuse) in their staging run to reduce tail.
- We saw occasional CPU scheduling spikes on the orchestration node during a concurrent restart (2 events > 1s) — we'll recommend a small canary window and a dedicated orchestration instance during the test.

Attached: ttft_stream_results.csv (csv) with raw per-request timings. Next steps: schedule the customer staging run (we estimate 90–120 minutes to complete) and provide them the harness container.

- Marissa
Marissa Cole
Perf Engineer, Redwood Inference

Attachments: ttft_stream_results.csv (csv)

From: Elena Ruiz <elena@stellahealth.ai>
To: Aisha Rahman <aisha@redwood.inference.com>, Marissa Cole <marissa@redwood.inference.com>
Cc: Procurement <procurement@stellahealth.ai>, Stella Health SRE <sre@stellahealth.ai>
Date: Mon, 02 Aug 2027 13:44:00 -0700
Subject: Re: MAP request — baseline run and preliminary results

Thanks — this looks great and aligned to our expectations. A couple quick items before we lock:

1) Can Redwood provide a 72-hour rolling request log export (anonymized) during the canary window for our SREs to review? We need this for our audit/compliance checks.
2) Procurement asked for the mutual acceptance clause to reference the exact CSV we produce as the artifact for sign-off. Can you confirm you'll attach the run CSV and the MAP PDF to the HubSpot deal record?
3) Proposed staging run schedule: Tue 03 Aug, 09:00–11:00 PT. Can you confirm Redwood team availability for a short kickoff at 08:45 PT?

If all good I'll trigger procurement and legal to prepare the PO/terms (they may loop in an engineer for the 08:45 kickoff).

—Elena

On Mon, Aug 2, 2027 at 10:22 AM Marissa Cole <marissa@redwood.inference.com> wrote:
> Aisha / team — I ran a dry-run on our staging cluster using the harness configured to Stella's token histogram. Notes and top-line numbers below (attached full CSV). Test run details:
> - Model variant: redwood-open-13b-quant-v2 (streaming enabled)
> - Hardware: 2xA100 (40GB) equivalent pool, continuous batching disabled for first-token measurement, KV cache warm for 60% of sessions
> - Concurrency shape: steady 12 sessions, random jitter (±10%)
>
> Preliminary TTFT & streaming numbers (single-run):
> - TTFT p50: 68ms, p95: 198ms, p99: 410ms
> - Streaming inter-token (first-2) p50: 32ms, p95: 142ms, p99: 290ms
> - End-to-end (180 tokens) p50: 610ms, p95: 1060ms, p99: 1520ms
>
> - Marissa

From: Ben Carter <ben@redwood.inference.com>
To: Aisha Rahman <aisha@redwood.inference.com>, Marissa Cole <marissa@redwood.inference.com>
Cc: Elena Ruiz <elena@stellahealth.ai>, Procurement <procurement@stellahealth.ai>
Date: Mon, 02 Aug 2027 16:35:00 -0700
Subject: Re: MAP request — scheduling and HubSpot artifacts

Aisha — confirming Redwood availability for Tue 03 Aug 08:45 PT kickoff. We'll: 
- Provide the harness container and run instructions by EOD today
- Attach the final CSV and MAP PDF to HubSpot deal DEAL-2027-1187 after the joint review
- Provide the 72-hour rolling anonymized logs export during the canary window (we'll redact per your compliance template)

Marissa — please include the orchestration instance pin and recommended autoscale guardrails in the MAP PDF. Elena, procurement — we'll look out for the PO.

Ben Carter
Enterprise Sales Operations
Redwood Inference

--

1) Can Redwood provide a 72-hour rolling request log export (anonymized) during the canary window for our SREs to review? We need this for our audit/compliance checks.
2) Procurement asked for the mutual acceptance clause to reference the exact CSV we produce as the artifact for sign-off. Can you confirm you'll attach the run CSV and the MAP PDF to the HubSpot deal record?
3) Proposed staging run schedule: Tue 03 Aug, 09:00–11:00 PT. Can you confirm Redwood team availability for a short kickoff at 08:45 PT?

If all good I'll trigger procurement and legal to prepare the PO/terms (they may loop in an engineer for the 08:45 kickoff).

—Elena

On Mon, Aug 2, 2027 at 10:22 AM Marissa Cole <marissa@redwood.inference.com> wrote:
> Aisha / team — I ran a dry-run on our staging cluster using the harness configured to Stella's token histogram. Notes and top-line numbers below (attached full CSV). Test run details:
> - Model variant: redwood-open-13b-quant-v2 (streaming enabled)
> - Hardware: 2xA100 (40GB) equivalent pool, continuous batching disabled for first-token measurement, KV cache warm for 60% of sessions
> - Concurrency shape: steady 12 sessions, random jitter (±10%)
>
> Preliminary TTFT & streaming numbers (single-run):
> - TTFT p50: 68ms, p95: 198ms, p99: 410ms
> - Streaming inter-token (first-2) p50: 32ms, p95: 142ms, p99: 290ms
> - End-to-end (180 tokens) p50: 610ms, p95: 1060ms, p99: 1520ms
>
> - Marissa


From: Ben Carter <ben@redwood.inference.com>
To: Aisha Rahman <aisha@redwood.inference.com>, Marissa Cole <marissa@redwood.inference.com>
Cc: Elena Ruiz <elena@stellahealth.ai>, Procurement <procurement@stellahealth.ai>
Date: Mon, 02 Aug 2027 16:35:00 -0700
Subject: Re: MAP request — scheduling and HubSpot artifacts

Aisha — confirming Redwood availability for Tue 03 Aug 08:45 PT kickoff. We'll: 
- Provide the harness container and run instructions by EOD today
- Attach the final CSV and MAP PDF to HubSpot deal DEAL-2027-1187 after the joint review
- Provide the 72-hour rolling anonymized logs export during the canary window (we'll redact per your compliance template)

Marissa — please include the orchestration instance pin and recommended autoscale guardrails in the MAP PDF. Elena, procurement — we'll look out for the PO.

Ben Carter

Redwood Inference

source.subject: Post-demo: who to loop for procurement & legal review + next steps
messages:
From: Aditya Rao <aditya.rao@redwoodinference.com>
To: Alex Johnson <alex.johnson@healthsync.com>
Cc: Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwoodinference.com>, Monica Patel <monica.patel@redwoodinference.com>
Date: Wed, 30 Jun 2027 16:15:00 -0700
Subject: Post-demo: who to loop for procurement & legal review + next steps

Alex — thanks again for your time this morning. Quick recap from our side and the asks we left on the call: 

1) What the team needs from us: low-latency inference for clinical note summarization (SLA target: <300ms p95), and an approach that keeps PHI in your VPC.
2) Items you said procurement / legal will want to review: SOC 2 (scope: production runtime), data residency and retention, standard DPA language, and an indemnity/time-to-notice clause.
3) Next steps we discussed: we provide SOC2 summary + SOW draft; you share names for the economic buyer + legal and procurement leads so we can set up intro calls.

Action items for HealthSync (can you confirm names/email + rough availability?):
- Economic buyer who owns the budget for model infra (we think a VP/Head-level owner)
- Primary legal contact for vendor review (contracts + DPA)
- Procurement contact to coordinate RFI/PO terms and timeline

From Redwood we will:
- Send a short mutual action plan and a draft SOW + SOC2 summary by EOD tomorrow.
- Loop Ben (solutions) on technical questions and Monica (CS) on rollout timeline/enablement.

If easier, feel free to reply with an org chart or a simple list — anything that helps us route the right people.

Thanks,

Aditya Rao
Account Executive | Redwood Inference
aditya.rao@redwoodinference.com | @aditya_rao

---
Attachment placeholders referenced: HealthSync_SOW_template_v1.docx (we'll send an editable SOW once you confirm owners)

From: Alex Johnson <alex.johnson@healthsync.com>
To: Aditya Rao <aditya.rao@redwoodinference.com>
Cc: Priya Shah <priya.shah@healthsync.com>
Date: Thu, 01 Jul 2027 08:03:00 -0700
Subject: Re: Post-demo: who to loop for procurement & legal review + next steps

Hi Aditya — great demo. Thanks for the clear recap. Quick answers below and a few clarifying Qs: 

Economic buyer: Heather Gallagher, VP Product <heather.gallagher@healthsync.com> (owns the budget for platform infra).
Legal: Priya Shah, Sr. Counsel <priya.shah@healthsync.com> (I looped her here).
Procurement: Marcus Nguyen, Procurement Manager <marcus.nguyen@healthsync.com>

Their initial asks: SOC 2 report (summary is fine to start), list of subcontractors, data flow diagram, and a short RACI for support/incident response. Procurement also wants a filled RFI template — attaching the file we typically use.

Timing: Heather will be at an offsite next week but available the week of Jul 12 for a 30-min decision conversation. Priya and Marcus are available next Tue/Thu mornings if you want to meet in the coming week.

Questions from our side: 
- Can Redwood support KMS integration for customer-managed keys (we'd prefer CMK in AWS KMS)?
- Do you offer a dedicated capacity option (isolated GPU pool) with an SLA?

I'll review the SOW once you send it.

Best,
Alex

---

> Thanks, Alex — looping procurement and ops later as needed.
> 
> Priya Shah
> Senior Counsel, HealthSync
